metadata.core_insight_en: This study investigates the individual factors influencing caffeine consumption, focusing on a large set of personality traits, socio-demographic variables, and smoking behavior. The results suggest that daily caffeine intake is higher in males, older individuals, and those who smoke more cigarettes, as well as those with higher impulsivity, sensation seeking, and reward sensitivity. However, the study also found that different patterns of individual variables predict caffeine consumption when considering the times of day and sources of caffeine.
metadata.core_insight_ko: 이 연구는 대규모 성격 특성, 사회인구학적 변수, 흡연 행태를 중심으로 카페인 섭취에 영향을 미치는 개인 요인들을 조사하였습니다. 결과적으로, 남성, 고령자, 흡연자, 그리고 충동성, 감각 추구, 보상 민감성이 높은 개인들의 일일 카페인 섭취량이 더 높게 나타났습니다. 그러나 시간대와 카페인 출처를 고려할 때는 개인 변수들의 패턴이 카페인 섭취를 예측하는 데 다양한 영향을 미치는 것으로 밝혀졌습니다.

text:
[Page 1]
ORIGINAL ARTICLE
The effect of black tea and caffeine on regional cerebral blood
ﬂow measured with arterial spin labeling
Rishma Vidyasagar 1, Arno Greyling 2, Richard Draijer 2, Douglas R Corﬁeld 3 and Laura M Parkes 1
Black tea consumption has been shown to improve peripheral vascular function. Its effect on brain vasculature is unknown, though
tea contains small amounts of caffeine, a psychoactive substance known to inﬂuence cerebral blood ﬂow (CBF). We investigated the
effects on CBF due to the intake of tea components in 20 healthy men in a double-blinded, randomized, placebo-controlled study.
On separate days, subjects received a single dose of 184 mg caffeine (equivalent to one strong espresso coffee), 2,820 mg black tea
solids containing 184 mg caffeine (equivalent to 6 cups of tea), 2,820 mg decaffeinated black tea solids, or placebo. The CBF and
cerebrovascular reactivity (CVR) to hypercapnia were measured with arterial spin labeled magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) before
and 2 hours after administration. We found a signiﬁcant global reduction with caffeine (20%) and tea (21%) in gray matter CBF, with
no effect of decaffeinated tea, suggesting that only caffeine inﬂuences CBF acutely. Voxelwise analysis revealed the effect of
caffeine to be regionally speciﬁc. None of the interventions had an effect on CVR. Additional research is required to conclude on
the physiologic relevance of these ﬁndings and the chronic effects of caffeine and tea intake on CBF.
Journal of Cerebral Blood Flow & Metabolism (2013) 33, 963–968; doi:10.1038/jcbfm.2013.40; published online 13 March 2013
Keywords: arterial spin labeling; brain; caffeine; ﬂavonoids; regional cerebral blood ﬂow; tea
INTRODUCTION
Tea produced from Camellia sinensis has been consumed for
thousands of years and is one of the most popular beverages
around the world. Epidemiologic studies have shown strong
associations between the long-term consumption of tea and a
reduced risk of stroke and cognitive decline.
1,2
These associations may be explained by the fact that tea and
the ﬂavonoid compounds found in tea have beneﬁcial effects on
endothelial function, a powerful predictor of cardiovascular
disease risk. 3 Flow mediated dilation of the brachial artery is a
measure of endothelial function and several intervention studies
have shown beneﬁcial effects of tea and other ﬂavonoid-rich
foods therein.
4,5 Small doses of ﬂavonoids, equivalent to one cup
of tea, already exert a substantial increase in ﬂow mediated
dilation.
6 A proposed mechanism is that ﬂavonoids improve the
bioavailability of nitric oxide (NO), a key mediator of endothelium-
dependent vasodilation, by stimulating activity of endothelial NO
synthase or by preventing superoxide-mediated NO breakdown.
It has been shown, in vitro, that tea and tea ﬂavonoids (theaﬂavins
as well as a fraction of black tea thearubigins) increase the
endothelial NO synthase activity by phosphorylation of the
enzyme.
7
Nitric oxide also plays an important role in the chemoregulation
of cerebral blood ﬂow (CBF). Changes in the arterial partial
pressure of CO 2 (pCO2) lead to vasodilation and increased CBF via
an NO-dependent mechanism. In humans, these pCO 2 changes
can be induced through mild hypercapnia, i.e., an increase
in the level of inhaled CO 2. Cerebrovascular responsiveness to
hypercapnia has been utilized with noninvasive imaging
modalities such as transcranial Doppler sonography
8 and
magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) 9 and is reduced in disease
states predisposing to increased risk of stroke and cognitive
decline.
Compared with the effects of tea and ﬂavonoids on peripheral
vascular function, little is known about possible effects on the
vasculature of the brain. Sorond et al. 10 did demonstrate that
consumption of cocoa, another rich source of ﬂavonoids,
increased the cerebrovascular responsiveness to CO 2 but further
research is warranted. We hypothesize that tea consumption
may enhance the CBF response to hypercapnia by affecting
NO bioavailability.
The MRI technique of arterial spin labeling (ASL) allows the
detection and quantiﬁcation of CBF and the blood oxygenation
level-dependent (BOLD) signal in the human brain. The ASL can
be used to continuously monitor CBF and BOLD during the
administration of a hypercapnic gas mixture, hence measuring
changes in CBF and BOLD linked to changes in end-tidal
CO
2 (ETCO2) levels (cerebrovascular reactivity or CVR). The BOLD
signal is complex, depending on changes in CBF, cerebral blood
volume, and oxygen metabolism, and is therefore not as easy to
interpret as CBF measurements. However, the BOLD signal has
higher contrast-to-noise ratio than the ASL-derived CBF signal,
and hence may be more sensitive to a hypercapnic-driven
change in CBF.
In addition to ﬂavonoids, tea contains low amounts of
caffeine (20 to 30 mg per serving size).
11 Caffeine is a potent
methylxanthine, which stimulates sympathetic nervous activity
and increases vascular tone. 12 The vasoconstrictive properties of
acutely administered caffeine lead to an overall global reduction
in CBF as has been shown using MRI techniques.
13 To date, there
have been limited studies determining precisely how caffeine
interacts with cortical perfusion on a regional level. Regulation of
1Centre for Imaging Sciences, Institute of Population Health, University of Manchester, Manchester, UK; 2Unilever R&D, Vlaardingen, The Netherlands and 3School of Medicine,
University of Manchester, Manchester, UK. Correspondence: Dr R Vidyasagar, Biomedical Imaging Institute, University of Manchester, Manchester, UK.
E-mail: rishma.vidyasagar@manchester.ac.uk
This study was funded by Unilever R&D Vlaardingen.
Received 3 September 2012; revised 17 January 2013; accepted 13 February 2013; published online 13 March 2013
Journal of Cerebral Blood Flow & Metabolism (2013) 33, 963–968

[Page 3]
model that assumes a single blood compartment model, i.e., assuming that
the labeled water does not cross the capillary wall or leave the voxel
during the TI.

metadata.core_insight_en: This study investigates the effects of black tea and caffeine on regional cerebral blood flow (CBF) in 20 healthy men. The results show a significant global reduction in gray matter CBF after consuming caffeine and black tea, but no effect on cerebrovascular reactivity to hypercapnia. The findings suggest that only caffeine influences CBF acutely, and further research is needed to determine the physiologic relevance of these findings.
metadata.core_insight_ko: 이 연구는 건강한 남성 20명을 대상으로 검은색 차와 카페인이 뇌 혈류량(CBF)에 미치는 영향을 조사했습니다. 결과적으로 카페인 섭취 후 회색질 CBF에서 전반적인 감소가 관찰되었으나, 과탄산혈증에 대한 뇌혈관 반응성에는 영향이 없었습니다. 이는 카페인만이 급성 CBF에 영향을 미친다는 것을 시사하며, 이러한 발견의 생리학적 의미를 밝히기 위한 추가 연구가 필요함을 나타냅니다.

during the TI.
23 The equilibrium magnetization of arterial blood was
estimated from the average signal of the M 0 calibration image, assuming a
whole-brain value of 0.9 for the brain:blood partition coefﬁcient ( l).24
Correcting for the different T2* relaxation times of blood and tissue,
perfusion maps were produced using equation 1:

seconds is the T 1 of blood, 25 T2b* ¼ 100 ms, and T2 t* ¼ 50 ms are the
approximate T2* of fully saturated arterial blood and tissue, respectively,
and S
0 is the whole-brain equilibrium tissue magnetization.
The T1-weighted image was normalized to the Montreal Neurological
Institute template within SPM and the same transformation was applied to
the coregistered CBF images. Gray matter was segmented on the
normalized T1-weighted image on an individual basis and CBF values
from gray matter were extracted.
Hypercapnia-Driven Responses
Hypercapnia ASL data were processed using in-house MATLAB routines to
separate the BOLD and CBF data components: Pairwise label and control
images were added to give a temporal series of BOLD images and
subtracted and converted into CBF images as described above to give a
temporal series of CBF images. Both BOLD and CBF data were then analy-
zed using Brainvoyager (Brainvoyager QX, Brain Innovation, Maastricht,
the Netherlands). Preprocessing consisted of motion correction using a
six parameter estimation incorporating three translation and rotation
parameters. Spatial smoothing was performed using a 6-mm Gaussian
kernel. Temporal smoothing was applied to CBF data with a full width at
half maximum of 30 seconds to help reduce temporal noise in the CBF time
course. A gray-matter region was deﬁned for each subject by segmenting
the T1-weighted image within BrainVoyager. To allow direct comparison of
the perfusion changes with brain anatomy, the BOLD and CBF images were
coregistered to the T1-weighted image and mean BOLD and CBF time
courses were extracted from all voxels within the gray-matter mask.
These time courses were then exported and used to calculate
CVR. Percentage BOLD and CBF changes were calculated from the ﬁnal
3-minute period of hypercapnia relative to the 3 minute baseline period.
The ETCO
2 values were extracted from the gas traces and converted into
units of mm Hg using atmospheric pressure readings from Liverpool
University vicinity on the day of the scan. Change in ETCO
2 (in units of
mm Hg) was calculated from the 3-minute period of hypercapnia relative
to the 3-minute baseline period. The CVR was calculated according to
equation 2:
CVRCBF ¼ DCBF
DETCO2
and CVR BOLD ¼ DBOLD
DETCO2
½2/C138
Statistical Analysis
Statistical tests were all performed on all three outcome measures (resting
CBF, CVR CBF, and CVR BOLD) in gray matter using the MATLAB statistical
toolbox. ANOVA was performed on the ‘after intervention’ data with
‘subject’, ‘intervention type’, and the ‘data before intervention’ as factors.
Age, height, weight, and body mass index were included as covariates in
the model. None of the covariates explained any of the variance in the data
and so were subsequently removed. Pairwise comparisons were performed
and the least squares mean difference between each intervention type was
recorded, along with 95% conﬁdence interval.
In addition, for the resting CBF maps, voxelwise analysis was performed
to determine regional effects of caffeine and ﬂavonoids using the PET
toolbox in SPM8 (www.ﬁl.ion.ucl.ac.uk/spm). A factorial ANOVA design was
used, considering the effects of time and intervention. The main effect of
caffeine was determined using the contrast of (caffeine /C0 placebo) þ
(tea /C0 decaff) for the ‘postintervention’ CBF images. The main effect of
ﬂavonoids was determined using the contrast of (decaff /C0 placebo) þ
(tea /C0 caffeine) for the ‘postintervention’ CBF images. Finally, any interac-
tion between caffeine and ﬂavonoids was determined by considering the
contrast ((caffeine /C0 placebo) /C0 (tea /C0 decaff)). For all contrasts, the effects
in both directions (i.e., increases or decreases in CBF) were considered. In
each voxel, differences in CBF signal between conditions were considered
to be signiﬁcant if Po0.05, corrected for the multiple statistical
comparisons across all voxels using Familywise error as implemented in
SPM. The minimum cluster size was set to 10 to avoid spurious signal
change in isolated voxels. Regions showing signiﬁcant differences were
identiﬁed by overlaying onto the anatomical automatic labeling atlas.
26
RESULTS
Resting Cerebral Blood Flow
Mean preintervention (baseline) gray matter resting CBF levels for
all four scan days across subjects remained relatively unchanged
(mean CBF: 37.6 ±4.8 mL/100 mL per minute). Figure 1 shows the
relative changes in resting CBF levels at baseline and after the
different interventions. Caffeine signiﬁcantly reduced gray matter
resting CBF by 20% compared with placebo. Tea also produced
signiﬁcant reduction in CBF of 21%. Decaffeinated tea had no
signiﬁcant effect on resting CBF. Finally, there was no signiﬁcant
difference in resting CBF between tea and caffeine, indicating
that caffeine was solely responsible for the observed reduction in
CBF caused by tea (Table 1).
Regional differences in CBF were observed using voxel-based
analysis performed in SPM. The main effect of caffeine was
reduced CBF in regions encompassing subcortical gray matter
(thalamus, basal ganglia, hippocampus, and posterior cingulum)
and isolated frontal, parietal, and occipital regions (Figure 2;
Table 2). There were no regions of signiﬁcantly increased CBF due
to caffeine. There were no signiﬁcant effects of ﬂavonoids on
CBF, nor any interaction between caffeine and ﬂavonoids.
These regional differences survived statistical thresholding, while
unthresholded t-score maps of the response to caffeine showed
widespread reduction in CBF.
Hypercapnia-Driven Responses
Baseline CBF and BOLD measures across all subjects showed a
strong response to hypercapnia, with an increase in gray-matter
CBF of 18% averaged across all subjects and sessions, and a BOLD
signal increase of 2.3%. Average changes in ETCO
2 of 7.9 mm Hg
resulted in mean baseline CVR measurements of 3.6%/mm Hg
(CVRCBF) and 0.45%/mm Hg (CVR BOLD).

The results show a significant global reduction in gray matter CBF after consuming caffeine and black tea, but no effect on cerebrovascular reactivity to hypercapnia. The findings suggest that only caffeine influences CBF acutely, and further research is needed to determine the physiologic relevance of these findings.

CBF, nor any interaction between caffeine and ﬂavonoids.
These regional differences survived statistical thresholding, while
unthresholded t-score maps of the response to caffeine showed
widespread reduction in CBF.
Hypercapnia-Driven Responses
Baseline CBF and BOLD measures across all subjects showed a
strong response to hypercapnia, with an increase in gray-matter
CBF of 18% averaged across all subjects and sessions, and a BOLD
signal increase of 2.3%. Average changes in ETCO
2 of 7.9 mm Hg
resulted in mean baseline CVR measurements of 3.6%/mm Hg
(CVRCBF) and 0.45%/mm Hg (CVR BOLD). Hypercapnia was well
tolerated and pulse rate was unaffected (average pulse rate
across all subjects: 63.9 ±8.6 b.p.m. before hypercapnia,
63.9±9.0 b.p.m. during hypercapnia). However, no signiﬁcant
effect of any of the interventions was seen on the CVR
CBF or
CVRBOLD measures (Table 1). Reproducibility of the CVR measures
between visits was calculated for all subjects. This was done using

interventions from global gray matter. Gray bars represent CBF

The effect of black tea and caffeine on CBF

965

[Page 4]
the preintervention scans for each subject across the four different

measures as a percentage of the mean measures, it was observed
that the baseline CBF measure had the best reproducibility at 14%,

CBF and CVRBOLD measures which was 101%
and 41%, respectively.
DISCUSSION
This study used advanced magnetic resonance techniques to
measure changes in CBF possibly linked to caffeine and other tea
components. Baseline CBF measures and a hypercapnic-driven
CBF and BOLD response were the key means of probing the
cerebrovascular system, and establishing any inﬂuence of tea
(components). The hypercapnia intervention was introduced in
the paradigm as a means of increasing the sensitivity of detecting
possible changes in blood ﬂow linked to the inﬂuence of tea on
the endothelial NO system. A novel ﬁnding of this study was
the observation of regional-speciﬁc CBF changes in response to
caffeine intake.
Resting Cerebral Blood Flow
Resting CBF is reduced after both caffeine and tea interventions
(Table 1; Figure 1). These reductions are found to be regionally
speciﬁc (Figure 2). The similarity of the results between tea and
caffeine, and the lack of any change in CBF after decaffeinated tea
(Table 1; Figure 1), suggest that these changes are primarily due to
the presence of caffeine. Indeed, the voxel-based analysis showed
no main effect of ﬂavonoids and no interaction between
ﬂavonoids and caffeine (Table 2; Figure 2), conﬁrming that the
global changes are entirely due to the presence of caffeine in tea.
Caffeine shows a widespread reduction in CBF, that is exaggerated
in key regions (Figure 2; Table 2). A reduction in CBF due to
caffeine is well documented in past magnetic resonance
and positron emission tomography studies,
27,28 however, to our
knowledge the regional speciﬁcity of these changes has not
previously been reported. The CBF was reduced in widespread
regions spanning primarily subcortical gray matter (thalamus,
basal ganglia, hippocampus, and posterior cingulum) and isolated
frontal, parietal, and occipital regions. Previous studies that have
focused on the effects of caffeine on CBF have primarily used it in
conjunction with a clinical cohort
29 or with a sensory stimulus 30 as
part of the paradigm. It is thus difﬁcult, from these preexisting
studies, to ascertain direct effects of caffeine on the normal
baseline neurophysiology. In the present study, we aimed to
reduce as many confounds as possible through a strictly
controlled design as far as dietary constraints and potential
confounds such as body mass index are concerned.
The observation of region-speciﬁc reductions in CBF after
caffeine is very interesting as they provide information on the
mechanism of action. We see both a general reduction in whole
brain CBF and isolated bilateral regions showing a much larger
response (Figure 2). This suggests there may be a global vascular
mechanism (driving the global change) as well as a regionally
speciﬁc neuronal mechanism. These regional reductions in CBF
due to caffeine may be due to its action as an adenosine
antagonist. A study applying an adenosine A2a antagonist in
patients with Parkinson’s disease
31 has revealed areas of similar
CBF change, with the thalamus showing the most signiﬁcant
Table 1.

patients with Parkinson’s disease

[Page 5]
change. Additional support for this argument can be found in a
study32 that used positron emission tomography imaging in
conjunction with an adenosine-sensitive tracer to identify the
locations of adenosine A2a receptors. Regions found in this and
other studies
33 are similar to regions showing reduced CBF after
caffeine administration (Figure 2; Table 2).
It is difﬁcult to be certain whether the regionally speciﬁc
changes in CBF are directly attributed to neuronal inﬂuence or are
acting on the vascular adenosine pathways based purely on
measures using MRI. The bilateral nature of the regional changes
observed and the similarities in activation patterns to studies
using adenosine antagonists are suggestive that the A2a receptor
which is neuronally implicated is involved. Adenosine has a role in
neuronal inhibition as well as in modulating the excitatory
neurotransmitter glutamate.
34 Considering the link between
glutamate and the A2a receptor that has been established in
animal model studies discussed earlier and the evidence that
caffeine interacts more heavily on the A2a receptor it is likely that
the neuronal receptors are playing a role in these changes.
It would be useful in future studies to address this point more
directly with tools sensitive to neuronal function such as
magnetoencephalography. The role of caffeine on mental states
has been established in past studies,
35 which show that caffeine
can be linked to anxiety, addiction, and excitement.
The decaffeinated intervention showed no signiﬁcant CBF
change on comparison with placebo (Table 1; Figure 1). Neither
were there any signiﬁcant changes in CBF due to the administra-
tion of ﬂavonoids or any interaction between ﬂavonoids and
caffeine. However, it cannot be deﬁnitively concluded that
components found in black tea are not able to affect brain
physiology. It is possible that the decaffeination process could
contribute to changes in other compounds found in tea,
so altering their action, or that the changes in CBF were too
small to be detected in our study. The size and presence of effects
might also be dependent on the (vascular) health status of the
study population. Also, data on chronic tea intervention are
lacking. Finally, the participants in this study were all regular tea
drinkers which may have blunted any acute effect of tea beyond
the caffeine response.
Hypercapnia-Driven Responses
The use of a hypercapnia intervention was chosen to mimic a
condition of increased demand of blood ﬂow in the brain, partly
mediated via the vasodilatory effects of NO. We found an increase
in CBF and BOLD due to increased CO
2 inhalation, in agreement
with other studies. 36 The CVR calculations enable alterations in
ETCO2 to be accounted for in the BOLD and CBF responses. The
CVR measures were not altered after any of the interventions,
indicating no effect of caffeine or other tea compounds. This is in
agreement with a previous study showing no change in CVR due
to caffeine.
27 While we show that caffeine and tea signiﬁcantly
reduce baseline CBF, CVR is not affected, suggesting that the two
mechanisms of blood ﬂow response (i.e., to hypercapnia and
caffeine) do not interact.
We hypothesized that tea would increase the hypercapnia-
driven CBF response.

metadata.core_insight_en: This study investigates the effects of black tea and caffeine on regional cerebral blood flow (CBF) in 20 healthy men. The results show a significant global reduction in gray matter CBF after consuming caffeine and black tea, but no effect on cerebrovascular reactivity to hypercapnia. The findings suggest that only caffeine influences CBF acutely, and further research is needed to determine the physiologic relevance of these findings.
metadata.core_insight_ko: 이 연구는 20명의 건강한 남성을 대상으로 검정차와 카페인 섭취가 뇌 혈류량에 미치는 영향을 조사하였으며, 결과적으로 카페인 섭취만이 회색질 뇌 혈류량에 일시적인 감소 효과를 보임을 확인하였고, 이는 추가적인 생리학적 연구의 필요성을 제기한다.

text:
We found an increase
in CBF and BOLD due to increased CO
2 inhalation, in agreement
with other studies. 36 The CVR calculations enable alterations in
ETCO2 to be accounted for in the BOLD and CBF responses. The
CVR measures were not altered after any of the interventions,
indicating no effect of caffeine or other tea compounds. This is in
agreement with a previous study showing no change in CVR due
to caffeine.
27 While we show that caffeine and tea signiﬁcantly
reduce baseline CBF, CVR is not affected, suggesting that the two
mechanisms of blood ﬂow response (i.e., to hypercapnia and
caffeine) do not interact.
We hypothesized that tea would increase the hypercapnia-
driven CBF response. We do not ﬁnd evidence for this, but
there are a number of limitations in our study: (1) the
reproducibility of the CVR responses was found to be quite
poor, limiting the change in CVR that we could have been
sensitive to (2) hypercapnia could potentially have caused a
change in CBF to a maximal physiologic level, such that any
further effects imposed by the presence of ﬂavonoids would have
been seen. This seems unlikely however as we used moderate
levels of hypercapnia, which would not have saturated the CBF
response.
37
It is difﬁcult to conclude if the observed reduction in CBF has
positive or negative physiological consequences, and the rele-
vance of these acute effects for habitual tea drinking remains to
be revealed. It is important to bear in mind that the tested single
dose was equivalent to B6 cups of ‘normal’ tea. The much lower
dose of caffeine consumed per cup of tea will most likely show
much less dramatic changes in CBF.
Clearly, the tea components other than caffeine, such as the
ﬂavonoids, appear to neither affect CBF nor counteract the
caffeine-induced CBF changes. The recent ﬁnding that one strong
cup of espresso may easily contain the amount of caffeine tested
in this study
38 does, however, raise questions about the
physiologic and cognitive impact of the observed CBF decline.
Tea is associated with reductions in the risk of stroke,
1 whereas the
evidence for coffee/caffeine is more ambiguous. 39 This could
imply that the reduction in stroke risk seen with tea is not
explained by effects on CBF but rather by effects on other risk
factors such as blood pressure.
40
In conclusion, while we ﬁnd strong, regionally speciﬁc reduc-
tions in CBF after caffeine administration, we do not ﬁnd evidence
for any effect of ﬂavonoids on CBF or CVR. It would be interesting
in future work to determine the link between regional CBF
changes and neuronal function and its impact on cerebral health.

text:
[Page 1]
Citation: Santos RM, Cotta K, Jiang S and Lima DRA. Does CYP1A2 Genotype Inluence Coffee Consumption?. 
Austin J Pharmacol Ther. 2015; 3(1).1065.
Austin J Pharmacol Ther - Volume 3 Issue 1 - 2015
Submit your Manuscript | 
www.austinpublishinggroup.com 
Santos et al. © All rights are reserved
Austin Journal of Pharmacology and 
Therapeutics
Open Access
Abstract
Coffee is the major source of caffeine in the American diet. Caffeine is 
95% metabolized by CYP 1A2, which has a polymorphic genetic binomial 
distribution within the population. The homozygous wild type confers a fast 
metabolizer phenotype and the homozygous variant allele confers a slow 
metabolism of caffeine, the latter being the least predominant in the normal 
population. Our study objective was to examine whether genetic variability of 
caffeine metabolism (CYP 1A2) is associated with plasma caffeine levels and 
can inluence the coffee consumption in young healthy volunteers. We found an 
inverse relationship between caffeine metabolization by CYP 1A2 and caffeine 
plasma levels at 45-60 minutes after intake of one cup of standardized brewed 
coffee (150 mg of caffeine). Our sample size was small (15 volunteers) but all 
possible genotypes were represented in accordance with the normal distribution 
in the population. We did not ind a correlation between CYP 1A2 genotype 
and frequency or type of coffee selection (caffeinated or decaffeinated). We 
concluded that a bigger sample size is needed in order to identify a probable 
inluence of caffeine metabolism phenotype on coffee consumption.
Keywords: Coffee consumption; Caffeine; Plasma levels; CYP1A2 
genotype
of the beans, method of preparation) [18]. he second one is related 
to individual genetic make-up in terms of cafeine pharmacokinetic 
metabolization (CYP1A2) and pharmacodynamic efects on 
adenosine receptors (A2A). Considering all the above mentioned it 
is tempting to hypothesize that individuals that tend to accumulate 
cafeine due to slow metabolization will present increased levels 
of cafeine in the body. hese in turn will be responsible for the 
adverse efects that could lead those individuals to abstain or avoid 
using cafeinated cofee. Another possibility would be to look at the 
individual intervariability on adenosine receptor, more speciically 
receptors A2A (ADORA2) and its possible consequences on cofee 
efects.
Objectives
he purpose of the present study is to examine whether genetic 
variability of cafeine metabolism (CYP 1A2) is associated with 
plasma cafeine levels and can inluence the cofee consumption in 
young healthy volunteers.
Mate rials  an d Me th o ds 
Subjects
Fiteen normal volunteers enrolled in the PharmD Program at 
South University School of Pharmacy, Savannah, GA. participated 
in this pilot research project. he study group is composed of twelve 
females and three males aging between 22 and 43 yrs. old and with 
body weights ranging from 150 to 250 lbs. he enrollment in this 
study was random with no speciic inclusion or exclusion criteria. We 
conducted a brief oral presentation in class explaining the purpose 
of the study to the students and collected signatures of possible 
interested participants. he subjects enrolled in this study signed an 
informed consent form that was previously submitted and approved 
In tro du ctio n  
he association between regular cofee intake and the risk of 
developing a number of disorders such as myocardial infarction [1, 2], 
hypertension [3-6], breast cancer [7] and Parkinson’s disease [8-10], 
just to name a few, has been the objective of various epidemiologic 
studies. All of the above mentioned studies mainly focused on the 
content of cafeine in cofee and considered cafeine to be the main 
ingredient responsible for the observed efects.
Cafeine is metabolized primarily by cytochrome P450 1A2 
(CYP1A2) in the liver through an initial N-demethylation [11,12]. 
CYP1A2 accounts for approximately 95% of cafeine’s metabolism 
and shows genetic polymorphism that relects the variability in 
enzyme activity between individuals [13]. 
An A→C substitution at position 734 (CYP1A2*1F) in the CYP1A2 
gene located at 15q24, decreases enzyme inducibility; as measured 
by the ratio of plasma or urinary cafeine to cafeine metabolites 
ater a dose of cafeine, resulting in impaired cafeine metabolism 
[11,12,14]. Carriers of the variant CYP1A2*1F allele are “slow” 
cafeine metabolizers, whereas individuals who are homozygous for 
the CYP1A2*1A allele are “fast” cafeine metabolizers [11,12,14]. 
However, it is not clear whether CYP 1A2 genotype alters cafeine 
consumption and prevents slow metabolizers from drinking cofee 
regularly. 
he fact that cafeine is one of the most widely used CNS 
stimulant and cofee the major source of cafeine in our diet, probably 
led those who are very sensitive to the efects of cafeine/cofee, to 
use decafeinated cofee or to simply reduce or even abstain from 
cofee intake. Additionally, the levels of cafeine in the body ater 
cofee intake are highly variable due to at least two main reasons [15-
17] . he irst is related to the cofee preparation itself (type/origin

Coffee preparation
One hundred grams of ground, medium roast cofee was brewed 
using 1.5 L of spring water under conditions set at temperature 
controlled brewer system BrewWISE (BUNN).
Sample collection
On the day of the study, the volunteers arrived at school having 
fasted for at least 8 hours. he protocol involved the collection of two 
time-point blood samples. he irst sample was time-point zero (t 0) 
collected as soon as they arrived.

metadata.core_insight_en: The study investigated the relationship between CYP1A2 genotype and plasma caffeine levels in young healthy volunteers. Results showed an inverse relationship between caffeine metabolization and caffeine plasma levels. However, no correlation was found between CYP1A2 genotype and coffee consumption frequency or type. The study suggested that a larger sample size is needed to identify a probable influence of caffeine metabolism phenotype on coffee consumption.
metadata.core_insight_ko: 연구는 젊고 건강한 자원자들을 대상으로 CYP1A2 유전자형과 혈장 카페인 수치 간의 관계를 조사했습니다. 결과적으로 카페인 대사율과 혈장 카페인 수치 사이에는 역의 관계가 나타났으나, CYP1A2 유전자형과 커피 섭취 빈도나 종류 사이에는 상관관계가 확인되지 않았습니다. 더 큰 표본 크기의 연구가 필요함을 시사하며, 카페인 대사형이 커피 섭취에 미치는 잠재적 영향을 파악하기 위함입니다.

[Page 2]

Santos RM Au s tin  Pu blis h in g Gro u p
Submit your Manuscript | www.austinpublishinggroup.com
by the Institutional Review Board of South University.
Coffee preparation
One hundred grams of ground, medium roast cofee was brewed 
using 1.5 L of spring water under conditions set at temperature 
controlled brewer system BrewWISE (BUNN).
Sample collection
On the day of the study, the volunteers arrived at school having 
fasted for at least 8 hours. he protocol involved the collection of two 
time-point blood samples. he irst sample was time-point zero (t 0) 
collected as soon as they arrived. he second sample (t 1) was collected 
between 45-60 minutes ater the administration of one cup of 200 
ml of a standardized brewed cofee, followed by a light breakfast 
composed of: a bagel with cheese spread, a muin and a cookie. he 
irst blood sample was collected with EDTA and used to harvest DNA 
for genotyping. he second blood sample was collected with heparin 
and used to determine the plasma cafeine levels ater acute cofee 
intake.
DNA preparation and CYP1A2 genotyping
Two ml of whole blood collected in EDTA tubes were used for 
genotyping. he blood sample was centrifuged at 14,300 g for 4 min 
at 20oC to separate the plasma from blood cells, which were used for 
DNA isolation by standard procedures (ArchivePure DNA Blood Kit, 
cat#2300740, 5-Prime, Gaithersburg, MD). Ater the inal step of DNA 
hydration, the samples were kept at 4 oC for short-term storage until 
the determination of the genotype. he DNA purity was ascertained 
through nucleic acid concentration determination, utilizing the 
absorbance spectra of 220-320 nm and calculating the ratio of A260/
A280 as a measure of purity (NanoDrop 2000C, hermo Scientiic, 
Wilmington, DE).
he CYP1A2 polymorphism was detected by real-time restriction-
fragment length polymorphism-polymerase chain reaction (TaqMan 
Drug Metabolism Genotyping Assay, Applied Biosystems, Carlsbad, 
CA). he assay was run in duplicates (Applied Biosystems 7900HT 
Fast Real-Time PCR System) at the Hoskins Cancer Center, Mercer 
School of Medicine laboratories in Savannah, GA. Figure 1 depicts the 
rationale of assigning the speciic alleles for each sample according 
with the luorophore that is released during the PCR reaction. In 
our method VIC™ dye luoresces when Allele 1 is present, which 
corresponds to the variant allele CYP 450 1A2*1F; when FAM™ dye 
luoresces means that Allele 2 is present and corresponds to the wild 
type, most common form of allele, CYP 450 1A2*1A. he phenotype 
will depend on the presence of a heterozygous or homozygous pair 
of alleles. here is co-dominance for this trait so that homozygosis 
for the variant will determine a slow metabolizer and homozygosis 
for the wild-type will determine a fast metabolizer phenotype and the 
presence of both alleles will determine an intermediate phenotype.
Plas m a Caffe in e  Le ve ls  
Apparatus
he instrumentation utilized for chromatography included a 
High Performance Liquid Chromatograph Shimadzu (Model LC-
20AT, Prominence, Kyoto, Japan) equipped with a manual injection 
valve (Rheodyne Injector Model 7725i) and a sample loop of 100 
μL (cat# 57627, SUPELCO, Sigma-Aldrich), a variable wavelength 
detector (Shimadzu SPD 20A), degasser and computerized remote 
control (Shimadzu CBM 20A/Alite) and a reverse phase column C18 
particle size 5μm, internal diameter 4.6 mm, 25 cm length (Ascentis 
C18 HPLC column, catalog # 581325-U, Sigma-Aldrich) linked to 
a guard column (Ascentis C18 Supelguard cartridge, particle size 5 
μm, internal diameter 3.0 mm, 2 cm length, catalog # 581375-U). 
Peak areas and retention time were evaluated by EZstart Sotware 
and all injections were made with a 25-μL syringe (Model 702 
SNR, Hamilton Co, Reno, NV) ater iltration with Millipore ilter 
(MILLEX, Millipore Corp., Bedford, MA). he mobile phase was 
Figure 1: TaqMan Drug Metabolism Genotyping Assay. Two sets of primers with 2 different luorophores, each one for a speciic allele. Wild type represented by 
the presence of luorescent dye FAM and the variant represented by VIC luorescence. The right panel shows the distribution of the pair of alleles, according with 
the luorescence emission region.

Santos RM Au s tin  Pu blis h in g Gro u p
Submit your Manuscript | www.austinpublishinggroup.com
iltered through membrane ilter (type HAWP, 0.45 μm, Millipore 
Corp., Bedford, MA).
Reagents
Cafeine was obtained from Sigma-Aldrich (standard grade, code 
C1778); Sodium Acetate Tri-hydrate (Fisher Scientiic, HPLC grade); 
Glacial Acetic Acid (Fisher Scientiic certiied A.C.S.); Acetonitrile 
HPLC grade (Fisher Chemicals); MilliQ- Water (Millipore); Acetate 
Bufer pH 4.0, 10 mmol/ and Human serum (Sigma-Aldrich).
Standard curve and samples preparation 
A standard stock solution was made by weighing 10 mg of cafeine 
and dissolving it in 100 ml of acetate bufer 10mmol/L, pH 4.0. he 
primary standard concentration was of 100 μg/mL and then diluted 
with human serum to obtain an intermediate standard solution of 20 
μg/mL. An 8-time point standard curve was prepared by sequential 
dilution of the intermediate standard with human serum, ranging 
from 10 to 0.078 μg/mL. A blank was prepared with human serum 
and distilled water. All the standards were carefully vortexed for 
10” and centrifuged at 12, 800 g per 10 minutes and the supernatant 
injected in the HLPC apparatus ater iltration through membrane 
ilters (SLGV033RB, MILLEX 0.22 μm). Two hundred and ity 
microliters of plasma obtained from each of the blood samples of the 
15 healthy volunteers were brought to room temperature, vortexed 
for 10” and centrifuged at 12,800 g for 10 minutes. he supernatant 
was then iltered and 10 μL of the iltrate was injected into the HPLC 
apparatus. 
HLPC method
he method used was based on Blanchard J and col. [19].

Results showed an inverse relationship between caffeine metabolization and caffeine plasma levels. However, no correlation was found between CYP1A2 genotype and coffee consumption frequency or type. The study suggested that a larger sample size is needed to identify a probable influence of caffeine metabolism phenotype on coffee consumption.
metadata.core_insight_ko: 연구는 건강한 젊은 참가자들을 대상으로 CYP1A2 유전자형과 혈장 카페인 수치 간의 관계를 조사했습니다. 결과적으로 카페인 대사율과 혈장 카페인 수치 사이에는 역의 관계가 나타났으나, CYP1A2 유전자형과 커피 섭취 빈도나 종류 사이에는 연관성이 확인되지 않았습니다. 더 큰 표본 크기의 연구가 필요함을 시사하며, 카페인 대사형이 커피 섭취에 미치는 잠재적 영향을 규명하기 위함입니다.

text:
A blank was prepared with human serum 
and distilled water. All the standards were carefully vortexed for 
10” and centrifuged at 12, 800 g per 10 minutes and the supernatant 
injected in the HLPC apparatus ater iltration through membrane 
ilters (SLGV033RB, MILLEX 0.22 μm). Two hundred and ity 
microliters of plasma obtained from each of the blood samples of the 
15 healthy volunteers were brought to room temperature, vortexed 
for 10” and centrifuged at 12,800 g for 10 minutes. he supernatant 
was then iltered and 10 μL of the iltrate was injected into the HPLC 
apparatus. 
HLPC method
he method used was based on Blanchard J and col. [19]. Briely, 
standards and samples were scanned over 10 minutes at 273 nm 
wavelength. he mobile phase constituted of a 10mM acetic acid/
acetate bufer, pH 4.0, 85/15, v/v, bufer/acetonitrile. An isocratic 
HPLC run with a low of 1.0 mL/min and a background pressure of 
approximately 2000 psi. 
Standards in duplicate were injected for each of the 8-time points. 
he standard curve constructed with the average value produced a 
very accurate linear regression line and a slope with a r 2 = 0.9902 
(CV% 0.023 and SD% 3.58).
Re s u lts 
Plasma caffeine levels
he mean plasma cafeine level (n=15) was 0.4 mg/L, the levels 
ranging from zero or below the minimum limit of detection (LOD= 
0.01 mg/L) to 1.3 mg/L. he plasma cafeine levels found in this pilot 
study are in accordance with previous studies. hese studies indicated 
a range between 0.25-2.0 mg/L [20], 0.5-10.0 mg/L [21], 0.28-10.58 
mg/L [22], 0.05-4.94 mg/L [23] of plasma cafeine levels ater 
cofee (1-2 cups) or cafeine (200-350 mg) intake. A typical plasma 
chromatogram is shown on Figure 2. Peaks are numbered from 1-4, 
cafeine represented by peak 4 with a retention time of 5.5 minutes 
on average. he other 3 peaks, even though not standardized for the 
purpose of our study, are likely to represent the 3 main metabolites 
of cafeine: theophylline (4%), theobromine (12%) and paraxanthine 
(84%) [17]. Under the chromatographic conditions used in our study, 
they are eluted in the following sequence: theobromine, paraxanthine 
and theophylline (peaks 1-3, respectively) [19]. 
Caffeine metabolism and CYP 1A2 genotyping
Eleven out of iteen blood samples produced a satisfactory DNA 
quality to perform the genotyping assay. We found that 8 out of 11 
(73%) presented a homozygous genotype for the wild type allele 
(1A2 *1A), which translates into the fast metabolizer phenotype, 
the most common in the normal population [17]. Only 1 out of 11 
(9%) presented a homozygous genotype for the variant type allele 
(1A2 *1F), slow metabolizer phenotype. here were 2 out of 11 (18%) 
heterozygous genotypes that can be translated into intermediate

same sample spiked with 10 μL of standard solution of caffeine of 1 mg/L (B). Peak 1= theobromine, Peak 2= paraxanthine, Peak 3 (shoulder) = theophylline, Peak 
4= caffeine. Retention times are 3.7, 3.9, 4.2 and 5.5 respectively.

Santos RM Au s tin  Pu blis h in g Gro u p
Submit your Manuscript | www.austinpublishinggroup.com
metabolizers (Table 1).
H abitu al Co ffe e  Co n s u m ptio n 
he volunteers illed a small questionnaire during the breakfast 
prior to drawing the second blood sample. he questionnaire 
consisted of a few questions about the number of cups of cofee, 
and cofee drinking frequency; the type of cofee used, if cafeinated 
or decafeinated and the current use of any regular medication 
prescribed or over the counter. he results related to habitual cofee 
consumption are summarized on Table 1. Fity percent of the fast 
metabolizers were not cofee drinkers and the only slow metabolizer 
consumed 1 cup daily. 
Dis cu s s io n  
It is a common belief that people avoid drinking cofee because 
of its efects, even though they also refer to the aroma of cofee as 
something very pleasant [22]. he cafeine content in cofee is 
likely to be responsible for the side efects experienced, since the 
common complaints are hand shaking, increase in the heart rate and 
anxiety [15,24]. Such efects are typically related to central nervous 
stimulation, one of the important properties of cafeine [20]. 
Our study is the irst, from our knowledge, to conirm that 
individuals that have the polymorphic variant  genotype (1A2 *1F) 
for cafeine metabolism (CYP 1A2), which translate into slow 
metabolizer phenotype, displayed the highest plasma cafeine level 
ater administration of one cup of cofee. he results also indicated 
that 8 out of 11 (90%) of the volunteers had the wild type allele (fast 
metabolizer), which is the most commonly found in the normal 
population [15]. It is interesting to notice that the fast metabolizers 
showed great variability in their plasma cafeine levels, which ranged 
from zero (not detectable) to 0.67 mg/L. he two intermediate 
metabolizers had plasma cafeine levels of 0.11 and 0.39 mg/L and 
the slow metabolizer had a plasma cafeine level of 1.11 mg/L (see 
Table 1).
One of the limitations of this study was that as a pilot study we 
established a minimum of twelve healthy volunteers with 10-20 % 
more to account for any losses during the study (N=12 + 3). he 
rationale for selection of 15 students as sample size is based on the 
average number of healthy volunteers normally enrolled on phase I 
clinical trials [25]. For future studies, we intend to make the research 
method less invasive, more attractive to students to participate, and 
thus increase the sample size. In order to do that we decided to use 
saliva instead of blood to measure cafeine levels and buccal cotton 
swab as a way to obtain cells to harvest DNA for genotyping.
We could not ind a direct relationship between the presence of 
the CYP 1A2 variant allele and reduced intake of cofee/cafeine.

Results showed an inverse relationship between caffeine metabolization and caffeine plasma levels. However, no correlation was found between CYP1A2 genotype and coffee consumption frequency or type. The study suggested that a larger sample size is needed to identify a probable influence of caffeine metabolism phenotype on coffee consumption.
metadata.core_insight_ko: 연구는 젊고 건강한 자원자들을 대상으로 CYP1A2 유전자형과 혈장 카페인 수치 간의 관계를 조사했습니다. 결과적으로 카페인 대사율과 혈장 카페인 수치 사이에는 역의 관계가 나타났으나, CYP1A2 유전자형과 커피 섭취 빈도나 종류 간에는 연관성이 확인되지 않았습니다. 더 큰 표본 크기의 연구가 필요함을 시사하며, 카페인 대사형이 커피 섭취에 미치는 잠재적 영향을 규명하기 위함입니다.

text:
One of the limitations of this study was that as a pilot study we 
established a minimum of twelve healthy volunteers with 10-20 % 
more to account for any losses during the study (N=12 + 3). he 
rationale for selection of 15 students as sample size is based on the 
average number of healthy volunteers normally enrolled on phase I 
clinical trials [25]. For future studies, we intend to make the research 
method less invasive, more attractive to students to participate, and 
thus increase the sample size. In order to do that we decided to use 
saliva instead of blood to measure cafeine levels and buccal cotton 
swab as a way to obtain cells to harvest DNA for genotyping.
We could not ind a direct relationship between the presence of 
the CYP 1A2 variant allele and reduced intake of cofee/cafeine. A 
previous study [15] examined the relationship between slow cafeine 
metabolizer and habitual cafeine consumption and did not ind 
any association. Instead, they found a positive correlation with the 
polymorphic receptor of adenosine ADORA 2A, main target of 
cafeine action in the central nervous system. More recently, genome-
wide association analysis (GWAS) of cofee drinking, where cofee 
consumption was used as a model for addictive behavior, analyzed 
8 Caucasian cohorts (N= 18,176). heir data pointed to regions on 
chromosome 15 (15q24) of other single-nucleotide polymorphisms 
(SNPs) between the genes CYP1A1 and CYP1A2, but no association 
was found between variants of CYP1A2 [26]. At the turnaround of 
year 2014, a consortium was formed, named he Cofee and Cafeine 
Genetics Consortium, looking for genome-wide identiication of 
possible loci associated with cofee consumption. hey found within 
a population of European and African-American ancestry that 
six loci showed positive association with cofee consumption and 
two of them, AHR and CYP 1A2 are directly related with cafeine 
metabolism [27].
Co n clu s io n 
Our results did indicate an inverse relationship between cafeine 
metabolism (CYP 1A2) and plasma cafeine levels. However due to 
our small sample we were unable to detect a correlation between 
the genetic variability of cafeine metabolism (CYP 1A2) and the 
cofee consumption habit in young healthy volunteers. Nevertheless, 
the GWAS have the advantage of surveying a very large population 
allowing them to attribute relevance to a potential inluence of genetics

their indings but further studies with a larger population are needed 
to better understand the correlation between CYP1A2 genotype and

Ackn o w le dge m e n t
We appreciated the donation of the BrewWise brewer from 
BUNN, and we acknowledge the support of our librarian Valerie 
Yaughn, and her assistant Miriam Wing, whom were real partners 
providing us promptly with all the articles necessary to put the 
manuscript together.

metadata.core_insight_en: The study investigated the relationship between CYP1A2 genotype and plasma caffeine levels in young healthy volunteers. Results showed an inverse relationship between caffeine metabolization and caffeine plasma levels. However, no correlation was found between CYP1A2 genotype and coffee consumption frequency or type. The study suggested that a larger sample size is needed to identify a probable influence of caffeine metabolism phenotype on coffee consumption.
metadata.core_insight_ko: 연구는 젊고 건강한 자원자들을 대상으로 CYP1A2 유전자형과 혈장 카페인 수치 간의 관계를 조사했습니다. 결과적으로 카페인 대사율과 혈장 카페인 수치 사이에는 역방향 관계가 나타났으나, CYP1A2 유전자형과 커피 섭취 빈도나 종류 간에는 연관성이 확인되지 않았습니다. 더 큰 표본 크기의 연구가 필요함을 시사하며, 카페인 대사형이 커피 섭취에 미치는 잠재적 영향을 규명하기 위함입니다.

text:
[Page 1]
Journal of Islamabad Medical & D ental College (JIMDC); 2016:5(2):88-91 
 88 
 Original Article 
 
Effect of Caffeine Intake on Mastalgia 
Saleha Anjum1 , Shahid Mahmood2 Ahmad Raza3 
1Assistant Prof, Department of Surgery, Islamabad Medical & Dental College, Islamabad 
2Prof /HOD, Department of Surgery, Islamabad Medical & Dental College, Islamabad 
3Assistant Prof, Department of Surgery, Islamabad Medical & Dental College, Islamabad 
(Shaheed Zulfiqar Ali Bhutto Medical University, Islamabad) 
 
 
Abstract 
Objective : To assess the relation of caffeine intake with mastalgia. 
Subjects and Methods: This prospective study was conducted at 
Social Security Hospital and Ahmed Medical Center Rawalpindi. 
Seventy-eight females with mastalgia between the age of 16 and 35 
were included in the study. The pain in breast was assessed on a 
visual analogue scale before and after caffeine intake. Results were 
entered on SPSS version 16 and p value of < 5 was considered 
statistically significant value. 
Results: Most of the participants (69.2%) were unmarried in our 
study. Majority of the patients (64.10%) cyclical mastalgia. Out of 78 
patients, complete pain relief was seen in only 3(3.84%) patients, 
4(5.13%) reported decrease in pain, while 71(91.03%) patients 
reported no change in breast pain. Patients after being refrained from 
all kinds of caffeine intake showed no significant change in their 
pattern of breast pain.    
Conclusion: Our study showed no significance relation of breast pain 
with caffeine intake. This disproves the common belief of dietary 
restriction tool for management of mastalgia. 
Key words: Breast pain, Caffeine Mastalgia, Methylxanthine 
Introduction 
Mastalgia (breast pain) was described in the medical literature 
as early as 1829 and is a common complaint amongst 
women.1,2 Most of them describe premenstrual mild cyclic 
mastalgia that lasts for 1 to 4 days as normal. Recent 
population based, and breast clinic-based studies suggest  
that up to 70% of women less than 55 years’ experience breast 
pain of some level and different durations.3-5 Caffeine is 
widely consumed at different levels. It is a neurostimulant with 
methylxanthiene as its active ingredient. It is consumed in 
different forms most commonly in the beverages e.g. tea, soda, 
coffee, green tea and chocolate. Both the public and the 
scientific community has expressed concern about its adverse 
effects on human health and this has been reported in different 
studies published after a comprehensive literature search. It is 
suggested that among its several systemic effects, 
overstimulation of breast tissue may occur due to interference 
with adenosine triphosphate degradation by methylxanthiene.1 
Another probable theory is release of cortisol with caffeine 
consumption leads to increased prolactin release causing pain 
in breasts.  
Caffeine as a cause of mastalgia is a common concept among 
health professionals and females who undergo this experience. 
This relation is still available in different medical literature 
and many medical practitioners advise their patients with 
mastalgia to refrain from caffeine consumption. However, it 
has been observed by some that after many years of patient 
interaction with mastalgia, many of the patients show no 
significant symptom improvement with caffeine avoidance. 
This observation became the base of our present study to 
observe effect of caffeine withdrawal on mastalgia.   
Mastalgia is a common problem in women and according to 
different research studies, it is estimated to be experienced by 
around 30%-70% in general population.6,7 Majority of the 
females are between 35-45 age groups and almost all seek 
medical advice in fear of breast cancer. Although studies 
revealed that only 0.8% were positive for cancer and other 
studies showed mastalgia as not a primary presentation of 
breast cancer.8,9 Factors mostly associated with mastalgia are 
age, hormones, premenstrual syndrome, fibrocystic disease, 
duct ectasia, stress, smoking, caffeine, psychiatric disorders 
and less commonly malignancy.10-12 It is proposed that 
overstimulation of breast cells may occur due to interference 
with adenosine triphosphate degradation by methylxanthine 
and thus leading to mastalgia.1,13 
For decades, women all over the world have been advised by 
their health care providers to avoid caffeine and other sources 
of methylxanthine (e.g., tea, coffee, chocolate) as a first line 
treatment for breast pain. Current medical literature also 
supports this theory.14 On the other hand, discussions have 
been done all over the world on different medical forums 
regarding the role of restriction of caffeine in the above 
mention disorder. Our present study was also planned to see 
the relation of caffeine with breast pain.  
Subjects and Methods 
This prospective was conducted at Social Security Hospital 
and Ahmed Medical Center Rawalpindi over a period of two 
Corresponding Author: 
Dr. Saleha Anjum 
E-mail: salyhakhan22@gmail.com 
Received: May 31st 2016;      Accepted:August1 7th 2016 

[Page 2]
Journal of Islamabad Medical & D ental College (JIMDC); 2016:5(2):88-91 
 89 
years (from November 2013 to December 2015).  About 78 
females with mastalgia between the age of 16 and 35 were 
included in this study. Those with definite breast lumps, skin 
problems and Ca breast were excluded from the study All 
patients included in the study had mastalgia of varying 
severity. Patients were made to refrain from caffeine intake for 
3 months. They were advised not to take tea, coffee, green tea, 
soda drinks and chocolates during this time. Paracetamol tab 
500mg 2 tab for unbearable pain advised on as per required 
basis. The severity of pain was noted on a visual analogue 
scale (VAS, also called visual linear analogue). VAS is 
usually a horizontal line, 10 cm in length, anchored by word 
descriptors at each end Fig. 1. On this VAS, the 0 indicates no 
pain and 10 indicate very severe excruciating pain.  
 
 
 Fig 1: Showing Visual Analogue pain Scale 
 
Results 
Total 78 females of between the age of 16 and 35 groups with 
history of breast pain were included in this study (Table 1).  
Out of 78 participants, 54(69.2%) were single and 
24(30.76%) were married. In the group of 78 patients, 53 
(67.94%) patients were either students or working ladies. This 
showed that mastalgia was common amongst single and 
working women or students than married and housewives. 
Obesity was found in 21(26.92%) females, 6 (7.69%) patients 
were taking contraceptives which also proved that obesity and 
contraceptives are not a significant cause of mastalgia. 
Number of patients suffering from cyclical mastalgia was 
more 50(64.10%) than noncyclical mastalgia 28(35.89%). 
Majority of the patients 53(67.94%) had bilateral breast pain 
while 9(11.53%) had right sided and 16(20.51%) had left 
sided breast pain. Pain severity scores showed mild pain in 
7(8.79%) patients, moderate in 55(70.51%) and severe in 
16(20.51%) patients. This showed that patients with moderate 
to severe pain consulted and seeked medical help and those 
with mild pain were not much concerned (Table 2).  
Physical examination revealed that only 24(30.76%) patients 
with mastalgia had breast tenderness and 17(21.79%) had 
changes in breast size.

metadata.core_insight_en: This study investigated the relationship between caffeine intake and mastalgia (breast pain) in 78 females between 16 and 35 years old. The results showed no significant correlation between caffeine intake and breast pain, contradicting the common belief that caffeine restriction can help manage mastalgia.
metadata.core_insight_ko: 이 연구는 16세에서 35세 사이의 여성 78명을 대상으로 카페인 섭취와 유방통(mastalgia) 사이의 관계를 조사했으며, 결과적으로 카페인 섭취와 유방통 사이에 유의미한 상관관계가 없음을 보여주어, 카페인 제한이 유방통 관리에 도움이 된다는 일반적인 믿음을 반박했습니다.

text:
Number of patients suffering from cyclical mastalgia was 
more 50(64.10%) than noncyclical mastalgia 28(35.89%). 
Majority of the patients 53(67.94%) had bilateral breast pain 
while 9(11.53%) had right sided and 16(20.51%) had left 
sided breast pain. Pain severity scores showed mild pain in 
7(8.79%) patients, moderate in 55(70.51%) and severe in 
16(20.51%) patients. This showed that patients with moderate 
to severe pain consulted and seeked medical help and those 
with mild pain were not much concerned (Table 2).  
Physical examination revealed that only 24(30.76%) patients 
with mastalgia had breast tenderness and 17(21.79%) had 
changes in breast size. Majority of these patients 42(53.84%) 
had nodular breast tissue while 23(29.48%) had tense breasts 
and 13(16.66%) had soft breasts. Regarding caffeine 
consumption, patients were divided in three categories. Those 
who took caffeine, usually in the form of tea or coffee, once 
daily were 31(39.7%), twice daily were 20(25.64%) and more 
than twice were 29(37.17%). These figures showed that there 
was not much difference in the number of patients in three 
groups regarding caffeine consumption (Table 3). At the end 
of the trial, out of 78 patients, complete pain relief was seen in 
only 3(3.84%) patients, 4(5.13%) reported decrease in pain, 
while 71(91.03%) patients reported no change. We also found 
that patients refrained from all kinds of caffeine intake showed 
no significant change in their pattern of breast pain (Table 4). 
Furthermore, it was also seen that patients with persistent 
symptoms were not in one particular group of caffeine 
consumption but had different consumption rates. Hence data 
of study showed that abstinence from caffeine consumption 
did not result in a significant reduction in breast 
pain/tenderness.

*pearson chisquare test. P<0.05 is considered significant 
 
Discussion 
In our study, 61% participants were in between the age of 25 
to 45 years and 64% had cyclical mastalgia. Makumbi T and 
his colleagues conducted a study in Mulago National Referral 
and University Teaching Hospital, Kampala, Uganda on 168 
patients. He showed a higher percentage of cyclical mastalgia 
(82%) in Africa.14 Their frequency of cyclical mastalgia in the 
same age group was slightly higher than our study. A pioneer 
study which supports this concept was published by Minton et 
al. in 1979 was the apparent genesis of the “avoid caffeine to 
reduce breast pain” advice, and since then many thousands of 
women and their health care providers continue to believe and 
follow such recommendations.15 The relation of caffeine with 
breast pain in Minton's research inspired others to further 
study the topic, but most results were either inconclusive or 
were unable to prove Minton's results. Two large randomized 
controlled trials in this respect were carried by Ernster, Mason 
& Goodson, in 1982 and Allen & Forsberg, in 1987.16,17 Both 
The studies found that reducing caffeine consumption failed to 
result in any significant lessening breast pain or tenderness. 
These results are very similar to our study but how caffeine 
was included in the list of causative agents for mastalgia is still 
unknown but many researchers proved its significance as a 
causative factor in many studies.18 Some investigations did 
find associations between breast pain and premenstrual 
syndrome, fibrocystic breast disease, and caffeine intake.19 But 
caffeine as an independent factor affecting mastalgia has not 
been studied much in literature. 
Caffeine as not being a cause of breast pain has also been 
studied by researchers.20 Although some women find their 
pain is less when they reduce or cut out caffeine, studies have 
not been able to prove it.21. A study conducted on Thai women 
showed no differences in caffeine intake between mild and 
severe mastalgia as proven by or study as well.13 A study 
about mastalgia and its management at Mayo Clinic also 
showed no significant role of caffeine restriction for breast 
pain.22 Pain severity scores showed mild pain in 7(8.79%) 
patients, moderate in 55(70.51%) patients and severe in 
16(20.51%) patients. Most of our patients (70.51%) 
complained of moderate intensity pain. These results are very 
similar to other studies where more than 55% patients fall into 
moderate group.10  
Managing breast pain is neither easy nor targeted to one cause. 
Physicians are still unclear about its exact origin and causative 
factors. Hence its treatment is also multi targeted which 
includes reassurance, lifestyle changes, diet control (caffeine 
& fatty food), a step ladder of different medicine and very 
rarely surgery. Drug options used in the treatment include 
bromocriptine, nonsteroidal anti-inflammatory drugs, herbal 
fruit extracts, danazol, tamoxifen, luteinizing hormone 
releasing hormone (LHRH) analogues, gamma-linoleic acid, 
testosterone, evening Primrose oil, B6 and vitamin E. 23,24 
Another intervention under study these days is use of Flax 
seed. Some studies have shown promising results with their 
use.25 However, choosing the appropriate drug is usually 
difficult due to the diverse side effects of hormonal treatments 
and the lack of consensus regarding the most effective 
treatment method.26  
Conclusion 
Contrary to the common belief, our study failed to show any 
association of  discontinuation of caffeine and decrease in the 
pain intensity in our patients.  
 
Study Limitation 
The study period was relatively short in our study (two to three 
menstrual cycles). Nevertheless, further studies with longer 
duration of interventions should be conducted in order to 
investigate the continuation of the effectiveness of caffeine 
restriction. Moreover we did not observe decrease in intensity 
of pain in first, second and third cycles and further studies 
taking into consideration this factor should be conducted. 
Conflict of Interest 
This study has no conflict of interest as declared by any author.

222. 
2. Chase C, Wells J, Eley S. Caffeine and Breast Pain: Revisiting 
the Connection. Nurs Womens Health. 2011; 15(4):286-94.

text:
[Page 1]
https://doi.org/10.1177/0269881118817158
Journal of Psychopharmacology
 1 –9
© The Author(s) 2018
Article reuse guidelines: 
sagepub.com/journals-permissions
DOI: 10.1177/0269881118817158
journals.sagepub.com/home/jop
Introduction
Drug addiction exacts a heavy toll – on the individuals who 
suffer from it, their families, and the society in which they live. 
The National Institute on Drug Abuse estimates that, in North 
America alone, the costs related to crime, lost work productiv-
ity, and health care from abuse of tobacco, alcohol, and illicit 
drugs total US$712 billion (NIH, 2016). Drug addiction is 
notoriously difficult to treat, with high relapse rates following 
treatment reported for a range of drugs (Allsop and Saunders, 
1989; Dimeff and Marlatt, 1998; Gossop, 1989; Mulé, 1984; 
Prochaska et al., 1991). Given the global disease burden of 
addiction, it is important to identify interventions that can 
improve addicted individuals’ chances of quitting. Targeting 
the negative expectancies that can exacerbate withdrawal may 
offer a cheap and ethical means of improving the outcome of 
existing treatments.
Medical treatments typically have two components, the direct 
effects of the treatment itself (the treatment effect), and the 
effects of knowing that the treatment has been administered (the 
placebo effect) (Benedetti et al., 2003a, 2011). The type of pla-
cebo effect that is elicited by a treatment depends primarily on 
the beliefs that individuals hold about the likely effects of that 
treatment. These beliefs – known as expectancies – can be 
derived from verbal information, direct experience with the treat-
ment, and social observation (Bandura, 1977; Colagiuri et al., 
2015; Colloca and Miller, 2011).
Evidence from the placebo literature suggests that when 
someone expects discontinuation of a treatment to result in nega-
tive outcomes, these expectancies can cause negative placebo (or 
nocebo) effects (Benedetti et al., 2006; Colagiuri et al., 2012; 
Colloca et al., 2008; Flaten et al., 1999). For example, Benedetti 
and colleagues (2003b) interrupted the dose of intravenous mor -
phine given to patients recovering from throat surgery and found 
that those patients who were made aware that their dose was 
being interrupted showed a higher rate of increase in pain than 
patients who had their morphine interrupted surreptitiously.
When individuals addicted to a drug discontinue that drug or 
have their dose substantially reduced, they too expect negative 
effects, in the form of withdrawal symptoms (Huntley and 
Juliano, 2012; Zvolensky et al., 2018; Zywiak et al., 1996). 
Several studies have demonstrated that withdrawal symptoms are 
reduced when abstinent users believe they have consumed their 
drug of choice, independently of actual drug consumption 
The effect of dose expectancies on caffeine 
withdrawal symptoms during tapered dose 
reduction
Llewellyn Mills1,2
 , Robert A Boakes1
  and Ben Colagiuri1
Abstract
Background: Negative expectancies can exacerbate withdrawal symptoms via the nocebo effect. As such, information provided about dose reductions 
during attempts to taper a drug could contribute to withdrawal symptoms and increase the likelihood of relapse. The current study tested whether 
blinding participants to dose reductions during a supervised caffeine dose taper reduced these nocebo-induced withdrawal symptoms.
Methods: Three groups of moderate to heavy coffee drinkers had their dose of caffeine reduced (tapered) from 300 mg per day to 0 mg over the course 
of five days and reported withdrawal symptoms twice daily. Groups were given differing information about how much caffeine they were receiving. An 
Open Reduction group was given accurate information about dose reductions. A Blind Reduction group was given no dose information whatsoever. A 
Deceptive Reduction group was misinformed about dose, with instructions suggesting that the dose remained on 300 mg for three days then dropped 
to 0 mg.
Results: The Open Reduction group reported more pronounced caffeine withdrawal symptoms than the Deceptive Reduction group on the days with 
the greatest discrepancy between actual dose and informed dose, indicating a nocebo effect of open versus deceptive reductions. In addition, the rate 
of increase in reported withdrawal symptoms in the Blind Reduction and Deceptive Reduction groups was less than that of the Open Reduction group.
Conclusion: These results suggest that awareness of dose reductions during a dose taper can result in a nocebo withdrawal effect, and that removing 
this awareness can reduce withdrawal. This has important implications for standard supervised dose-tapering practice, where patients are aware of 
the timing and magnitude of dose reductions.
Keywords
Withdrawal, expectancy, addiction, caffeine, placebo, dose tapering, non-selective adenosine antagonist
1School of Psychology, University of Sydney, Sydney, NSW, Australia
