text:
3.2. T ension Type Headache (TTH)
According to the International Classiﬁcation of Headache Disorders, 3rd ed., a tension-
type headache is a recurrent headache disorder with episodes lasting from 30 min to
7 days. The typical characteristics of this headache are bilateral location, pressing or tensing
(non-throbbing) nature, mild to moderate intensity and no correlation of severity with
routine physical activity. It is not accompanied by nausea or vomiting, but photophobia or
phonophobia may occur (but never both at the same time) [15].

[Page 8]
Nutrients 2023, 15, 3170 8 of 16
A multicenter, double-blind, placebo-controlled parallel study by Diamond et al. on a
group of 301 patients demonstrated the efﬁcacy and safety of ibuprofen in combination
with caffeine in the treatment of tension-type headache. Comparisons were made between
ibuprofen alone, caffeine alone, ibuprofen plus caffeine and placebo. According to previous
data, caffeine showed analgesic properties, although the effects were not as signiﬁcant as
the combination of ibuprofen and caffeine [50]. In turn, in a study conducted in Italy on a
group of 93 patients with tension-type headache, a good safety proﬁle and tolerability of the
combination of paracetamol 1000 mg and caffeine 130 mg were demonstrated, compared to
naproxen 550 mg and placebo. Paracetamol with caffeine was well tolerated and effective in
the treatment of acute TTH [51]. In a meta-analysis by Diener et al. of studies on the use of a
combination of acetylsalicylic acid (500 mg), acetaminophen (500 mg) and caffeine (130 mg)
in the treatment of an acute episode of TTH, this combination of drugs was shown to be
well tolerated and effective in 28.5% of patients, while the effectiveness of acetaminophen
was revealed in 21% of patients, and in the case of placebo, only 18% of people in this group
improved [52].
4. The Role of Caffeine as the Main Component in the Treatment of Headaches
4.1. Hypnic Headache (HH)
According to the International Classiﬁcation of Headache Disorders, 3rd edition, a
hypnic headache is a recurrent headache that develops only during sleep and causes
awakening. The headache lasts from 15 min to 4 h after waking up. A diagnosis of hypnic
headache can be made if the characteristic headache occurs at least 10 days a month for
more than 3 months [15]. This type of primary headache usually starts after the age of 50.
Its prevalence among patients treated in headache centers is estimated at 0.07–0.35%, but
the exact prevalence of hypnic headache is unknown [53].
One of the typical features of HH is that caffeine often stops acute pain, but also when
used prophylactically before going to bed, it prevents the onset of pain [8]. Some authors
consider a good response to caffeine as a pathognomonic feature of HH [54]. It should be
added that experimental studies have shown that the administration of adenosine A2A
receptor agonists into the subarachnoid space may induce NREM sleep [55].
Due to the rarity of hypnic headache, treatment recommendations are based mainly
on expert opinions and small case series [56]. Based on the review of the available literature,
it seems that caffeine can be used both as a ﬁrst-line drug in the treatment of the acute
phase of HH and as a prophylactic agent. Almost all patients reported that drinking a
cup of strong black coffee for pain was effective in treating acute HH attacks at night.
In contrast, drinking a cup of coffee before going to bed effectively prevented headache
attacks. Caffeine-containing painkillers also seem to be effective in the treatment of hypnic
headache—patients typically report that the use of a caffeinated combination of painkillers
(e.g., paracetamol with caffeine) leads to signiﬁcant pain relief during an acute HH attack,
while painkillers alone do not stop the headache [8].
In a retrospective study by Tariq et al., 40 patients treated for HH in a reference
headache center were described. Of the 15 different drugs tried, the best response was
seen with lithium, with 70% complete response and 20% moderate response. In the case of
caffeine, a complete response was obtained in 28% of patients and a moderate response in
43% of patients [57].
With regard to hypnic headache, it can be said that the therapeutic response to caffeine
goes beyond the usual analgesic effect seen in other types of headache [8].
4.2. Post-Dural Puncture Headache (PDPH)
Post-dural puncture headache may be a consequence of a lumbar puncture performed
for diagnostic or therapeutic purposes, or may occur as a complication of epidural anes-
thesia. It rarely occurs in an intense form, but can be nagging [ 58]. According to the
International Classiﬁcation of Headache Disorders, 3rd ed., PDPH is included in the group

[Page 9]
Nutrients 2023, 15, 3170 9 of 16
of headaches attributed to low cerebrospinal ﬂuid pressure. Headache develops within
5 days of the dural puncture [15].
The pathogenesis of PDPH remains unclear, but it is assumed that it is probably a
consequence of leakage of CSF into the epidural space and reduction of CSF pressure.
There are two possible mechanisms. First, lowering the pressure of the cerebrospinal ﬂuid
causes traction of pain-sensitive intracranial structures in the vertical position, which causes
the characteristic headache. Second, the loss of cerebrospinal ﬂuid causes compensatory
venous dilatation (Monro–Kelli principle) responsible for headache [59]. The occurrence of
post-puncture headache is inﬂuenced by the needle size, direction of bevel, needle design,
replacement of the stylet and number of lumbar puncture attempts [ 3]. A characteristic
feature of PDPH is a reduced scale of pain in the lying position and its increase after
verticalization [60]. Methylxanthines, such as caffeine and theophylline, are some of the
best-studied medications for PDPH relief. These drugs act symptomatically in PDPH by
two mechanisms: they interfere with calcium uptake in the sarcoplasmic reticulum and
block phosphodiesterase and antagonize adenosine, which causes cerebral vasoconstriction,
and they increase the production of cerebrospinal ﬂuid by stimulating sodium-potassium
pumps [2].
Caffeine is known to have been used to treat PDPH as early as 1949 [ 61]. Several
studies and some case reports recommend oral and intravenous caffeine as a treatment
option, although headache recurrence after caffeine treatment is common [ 3]. In a ran-
domized, double-blind clinical trial by Yucel et al., 1000 mL of saline and 500 mg sodium
caffeine benzoate or 1000 mL of saline were administered within the ﬁrst 90 min after
spinal anesthesia. Caffeine administration reduced the incidence of PDPH [ 62]. How-
ever, Stevens et al. noted that the study did not take into account all the criteria for the
diagnosis of post-puncture headache and previous caffeine consumption by the subjects,
which is why some patients may have developed caffeine-withdrawal headache rather than
post-puncture headache [63].

metadata.core_insight_en:
Caffeine affects headaches through various mechanisms, and its consumption can both relieve and trigger headaches. 
                 It can also cause caffeine-withdrawal headache when abruptly discontinued. 
                 Moderate amounts of caffeine are safe for healthy adults, but high doses can have negative effects.
metadata.core_insight_ko: 카페인은 두통에 다양한 기전으로 영향을 미치며, 섭취는 두통 완화와 유발 모두 가능합니다. 갑작스러운 중단은 카페인 금단성 두통을 일으킬 수 있습니다. 건강한 성인에게는 적당량의 카페인 섭취가 안전하지만, 과다 섭취는 부정적 영향을 초래할 수 있습니다.

text:
Caffeine is known to have been used to treat PDPH as early as 1949 [ 61]. Several
studies and some case reports recommend oral and intravenous caffeine as a treatment
option, although headache recurrence after caffeine treatment is common [ 3]. In a ran-
domized, double-blind clinical trial by Yucel et al., 1000 mL of saline and 500 mg sodium
caffeine benzoate or 1000 mL of saline were administered within the ﬁrst 90 min after
spinal anesthesia. Caffeine administration reduced the incidence of PDPH [ 62]. How-
ever, Stevens et al. noted that the study did not take into account all the criteria for the
diagnosis of post-puncture headache and previous caffeine consumption by the subjects,
which is why some patients may have developed caffeine-withdrawal headache rather than
post-puncture headache [63]. In a study by Camann et al., 300 mg of caffeine orally was
administered to 40 postpartum PDPH patients and pain scores were assessed immediately
before drug administration, and after 4 and 24 h. Oral caffeine relieved the headache [64].
Esmaoglu et al., in a randomized, double-blind, placebo-controlled study of 210 otherwise
healthy patients scheduled for spinal anesthesia for elective lower limb surgery, failed
to demonstrate the efﬁcacy of a multi-dose combination of caffeine and paracetamol. In
the study, patients received a placebo, 500 mg paracetamol + 75 mg caffeine or 500 mg
paracetamol + 125 mg caffeine. Perhaps the doses of caffeine used were too low [65].
Studies in patients with PDPH have shown that the use of a single dose of 300 mg of
oral caffeine or 500 mg of intravenous caffeine relieves headache associated with this syn-
drome. A Cochrane review concluded that these doses of caffeine were better than placebo
in the treatment of PDPH, with fewer patients requiring additional interventions [42].
The administration of caffeine appears to be a non-invasive and safe option that
can replace the use of invasive methods, e.g., epidural blood patch [ 61]. The currently
recommended dose for the treatment of post-puncture headache is 300–500 mg of oral or
intravenous caffeine once or twice daily. However, therapeutic doses may be associated
with toxicity to the central nervous system (pathological agitation, anxiety) or the heart
(atrial ﬁbrillation), which should be taken into account [59].
4.3. Spontaneous Intracranial Hypotension (SIH)
Spontaneous intracranial hypotension is an important secondary headache syndrome
that has been increasingly recognized in recent years. Headache usually starts when stand-
ing and goes away when lying down (referred to as orthostatic headache) [66]. According
to the International Classiﬁcation of Headache Disorders, 3rd ed., headache attributed
to SIH is included in the group of headaches attributed to low cerebrospinal ﬂuid (CSF)
pressure. Headache occurs despite the absence of a procedure or trauma known to be able
to cause CSF leakage. It develops in temporal relation with the occurrence of CSF low
pressure or with CSF leak, or leads to its detection [15].

[Page 10]
Nutrients 2023, 15, 3170 10 of 16
It often has a sudden onset and is accompanied by other neurological symptoms:
dizziness, tinnitus, mental stupor, and behavioral changes, but clinical variability makes
diagnosis difﬁcult [67]. Although positional (usually tilting) headache is the most common
symptom of SIH, the positional factor may not be dominant, have variable intensity or
evolve. It can also be completely elusive [66]. Data on the exact epidemiology of idiopathic
intracranial hypotension are not available, but the annual incidence is estimated at 4–5 cases
per 100,000 population. Women aged 35 to 55 are most often affected [67].
SIH is caused by a leak of cerebrospinal ﬂuid and is potentially treatable if the leak can
be stopped [66]. Loss of cerebrospinal ﬂuid displaces brain structures, causing headache
and other neurological symptoms [67]. Most patients have normal CSF pressure, but there
is a reduced volume of intracranial CSF. This volume reduction is due to the leakage of
cerebrospinal ﬂuid through the meningeal membrane at one or more sites. It is usually
caused by one of three causes (etiologies): (1) leaks resulting from the weakening of the
dura mater involving nerve root sleeves, (2) tearing of the ventral dura mater associated
with disc herniation and (3) ﬂuid (csf)-venous ﬁstulas. Conservative treatment, which
includes bed rest, caffeine and hydration, is often recommended as the ﬁrst-choice therapy
for patients with SIH [66].
Caffeine is the most commonly used agent and can be given in the form of caffeinated
beverages or caffeine tablets [66]. Firstly, caffeine inhibits phosphodiesterase and antago-
nizes adenosine, leading to cerebral vasoconstriction; secondly, it increases the production
of cerebral ﬂuid by activating the sodium–potassium pump. If symptoms do not respond
to medical treatment, an epidural blood patch (EBP) may be considered [ 68]. In a meta-
analysis of studies by D’Anton et al., conservative treatment was effective in 28% of patients.
A single epidural blood patch was successful in 64% and large epidural blood patches
(>20 mL) had better success rates than small epidural blood patches [ 69]. Fibrin glue is
believed to act as a focal sealer at the site of the leak and is often used in conjunction with
EBP , less often as an alternative to EBP . Surgical treatment is considered when the site of the
leak has been identiﬁed and symptoms persist despite the use of less invasive methods [70].
5. The Role of Caffeine Withdrawal in Provoking Headaches
5.1. Caffeine-Withdrawal Headache
The International Headache Society does not list caffeine as a potential cause of
analgesic overuse headache, but rather as a substance that can cause headache when
regular consumption is abruptly discontinued. Caffeine-withdrawal headache, according
to the International Classiﬁcation of Headache Disorders, 3rd edition, is a headache that
develops within 24 h of discontinuation of regular caffeine intake of more than 200 mg/day
for at least 2 weeks. It resolves spontaneously within 14 days in the absence of further
consumption or within 1 h by intake of caffeine 100 mg [15].
High caffeine intake, especially in coffee, tea, cola and other types of caffeinated bever-
ages, may be a trigger factor for withdrawal headache when caffeine intake is discontinued.
The effect of caffeine varies from person to person, for example, due to different body
weights or different conditions of absorption, and depends on the developed phenomenon
of tolerance. Therefore, it is not possible to establish a uniform, maximum, safe threshold
for caffeine consumption [8]. Frequent consumption of caffeine may result in a behavioral
disorders called caffeine dependence syndrome, recognized by the WHO as a disease
entity. Addiction is caused by increased dopaminergic activity, especially by blocking
A2A receptors, resulting in increased dopamine release in the striatum [6]. The long-term
effects of caffeine abuse result from the overregulation and hypersensitivity of adenosine
receptors. This process may explain the pronounced physical dependence resulting from
long-term caffeine abuse.

metadata.core_insight_en:
Caffeine affects headaches through various mechanisms, and its consumption can both relieve and trigger headaches. 
                 It can also cause caffeine-withdrawal headache when abruptly discontinued. 
                 Moderate amounts of caffeine are safe for healthy adults, but high doses can have negative effects.
metadata.core_insight_ko: 카페인은 다양한 기전을 통해 두통에 영향을 미치며, 섭취는 두통 완화와 유발 모두 가능합니다. 갑작스러운 중단은 카페인 금단성 두통을 일으킬 수 있으며, 건강한 성인에게는 적당량이 안전하지만 과다 섭취는 부정적 영향을 줄 수 있습니다.

text:
High caffeine intake, especially in coffee, tea, cola and other types of caffeinated bever-
ages, may be a trigger factor for withdrawal headache when caffeine intake is discontinued.
The effect of caffeine varies from person to person, for example, due to different body
weights or different conditions of absorption, and depends on the developed phenomenon
of tolerance. Therefore, it is not possible to establish a uniform, maximum, safe threshold
for caffeine consumption [8]. Frequent consumption of caffeine may result in a behavioral
disorders called caffeine dependence syndrome, recognized by the WHO as a disease
entity. Addiction is caused by increased dopaminergic activity, especially by blocking
A2A receptors, resulting in increased dopamine release in the striatum [6]. The long-term
effects of caffeine abuse result from the overregulation and hypersensitivity of adenosine
receptors. This process may explain the pronounced physical dependence resulting from
long-term caffeine abuse. It is also fundamental to understanding withdrawal syndrome:
abrupt discontinuation of caffeine intake causes adenosine receptors to become available,
leading to vasodilation and a signiﬁcant increase in blood ﬂow to the brain. These physio-
logical changes are responsible for the caffeine-withdrawal headache [4]. Symptoms such
as headache, fatigue and impaired concentration appear between 12 and 24 h after caf-

[Page 11]
Nutrients 2023, 15, 3170 11 of 16
feine withdrawal and can occur with chronic caffeine consumption in doses ≥100 mg/day.
Withdrawal headaches in caffeine users correspond to increased cerebral blood ﬂow after
20–24 h of caffeine abstinence [9].
In an epidemiological study of 1741 people living in the Norwegian town of Vågå,
caffeine-withdrawal headache was found in only 7 people (0.4%) with relatively high coffee
consumption (average 4.7 cups per day). The coffee relieved that headache. The study
was completely retrospective, only looking at spontaneous caffeine intake and withdrawal
consequences, including headaches among many symptoms. Conclusions from this study
should therefore be drawn with caution [71].
In a review of studies on caffeine withdrawal by Juliano et al., the most frequently
assessed symptom was headache (48 experimental studies and 6 survey studies). Headache
was reported in 77% of the experimental studies that evaluated it. The median percentage
of people reporting headache in the experimental studies reviewed was 47%, ranging from
9% to 100% across various ones. In survey studies, the median percentage of caffeine users
reporting caffeine-withdrawal headaches was 24%, ranging from 8% to 56%. Abstinence-
induced headache has also been shown to be limited in time and resolve rapidly, and often
completely, upon re-administration of caffeine [34].
Postoperative Headache
Caffeine withdrawal may be one of the factors of the frequently observed perioperative
headache, as many operations require patients to fast, which for a signiﬁcant proportion
of patients means coffee-free days [72]. There are no randomized, controlled, multicenter
studies evaluating the administration of caffeine in the perioperative period. The results
of the few studies conducted suggest that the administration of caffeine in the perioper-
ative period reduces the incidence of headache associated with caffeine withdrawal [73].
Hampl et al. studied 40 patients who underwent minor surgery. Half of the patients were
treated with caffeine tablets, and the other half received only placebo tablets. In the placebo
group, 50% of the patients reported a headache after surgery, and no one in the caffeine
group had a headache [74]. In a prospective, randomized, double-blind clinical trial con-
ducted by Weber et al. in 300 adult patients, the prophylactic postoperative administration
of 200 mg intravenous caffeine reduced the incidence of postoperative headache in patients
at risk of caffeine withdrawal symptoms [75].
A review of studies by Bright et al. found that withdrawal from caffeine due to hospi-
talization is rapid; therefore, patients may experience withdrawal symptoms, including
headache. Of the seven prospective studies evaluating the effects of caffeine withdrawal,
ﬁve studies showed that caffeine withdrawal increased the incidence of postoperative
headache. Three studies have investigated the administration of caffeine for the relief
of postoperative headache, including two prospective randomized controlled trials that
concluded that the administration of caffeine prophylaxis reduces the incidence of postop-
erative headache [76].
6. The Role of Caffeine in the Development of Chronic Headache
6.1. Medication Overuse Headache (MOH)
Caffeine appears to increase the risk of developing MOH to a level similar to that of
ergot derivatives and opioids. Medication overuse headache, according to the International
Classiﬁcation of Headache Disorders, 3rd ed., occurs for≥15 days/month in a patient with
pre-existing headache and is associated with regular overuse for >3 months of one or more
medications that may be used for the treatment of primary or secondary/symptomatic
headache [15]. It is known that the risk of MOH is related to the frequency of caffeine
use [30], but its role in the pathomechanism of MOH remains unclear [8].
MOH is characterized by two main pathophysiological mechanisms: cortical hyperex-
citability and increased peripheral and central sensitization. Both of these phenomena may
be chronic effects of excessive caffeine intake: hyperactivity of the cerebral cortex is induced
by increased release of excitatory neurotransmitters (mainly glutamate), while the prono-

[Page 12]
Nutrients 2023, 15, 3170 12 of 16
ciceptive state is promoted by chronic activation of A2A receptors at the peripheral level.
These receptors promote the action of CGRP , a powerful pronociceptive neuropeptide that
plays a major role in the transition of headaches to the chronic phase [4]. A retrospective
clinical trial conducted at a headache center showed a positive correlation between daily
caffeine intake and MOH (increased odds ratio 2.2) as well as chronic migraine (increased
odds ratio 2.9) [77].
6.2. Chronic Daily Headache (CDH)
A population study by Scher et al. showed a possible relationship between dietary
caffeine intake and caffeine use in medicinal products and chronic daily headache. Caffeine
consumption for therapeutic and dietary purposes was measured in a group of people
suffering from episodic and chronic headaches, including migraine. High therapeutic or
dietary caffeine intake at the time of CDH onset was a moderate risk factor for chronic daily
headache. Secondary analyses showed that caffeine intake before CDH may be a trigger in
a subset of CDH sufferers [78].
7. Summary and Conclusions
The links between caffeine and headaches are multidirectional and multifactorial.
Caffeine can be both a trigger of a migraine attack, and its withdrawal can provoke a
headache (caffeine-withdrawal headache). Appropriately applied doses of caffeine sig-
niﬁcantly increase the therapeutic effect of common analgesics and NSAIDs in migraine
patients and patients with TTH. Conversely, overuse of caffeine-containing medications
may expose patients to overuse headache and lead to the development of a chronic form
of tension headache or chronic migraine. The important role of caffeine in the treatment
of certain types of headaches should also be emphasized: hypnic headache, post-dural
puncture headache and spontaneous intracranial hypotension. At the same time, the results
of an uncontrolled clinical trial suggest that stopping caffeine consumption may improve
the effectiveness of treatment of acute migraine [42].
Headache patients may be advised to continue drinking caffeinated beverages as
long as they consume low to moderate amounts of caffeine (preferably <200 mg/day)
and their daily caffeine intake is similar. If patients cannot consume a similar dose of
caffeine throughout the day and maintain a regular level, the best solution is to gradually
withdraw from caffeine.

metadata.core_insight_en:
Caffeine affects headaches through various mechanisms, and its consumption can both relieve and trigger headaches. 
                 It can also cause caffeine-withdrawal headache when abruptly discontinued. 
                 Moderate amounts of caffeine are safe for healthy adults, but high doses can have negative effects.
metadata.core_insight_ko: 카페인은 다양한 기전을 통해 두통에 영향을 미치며, 섭취는 두통 완화와 유발 모두 가능합니다. 갑작스러운 중단 시 카페인 금단 두통을 일으킬 수 있으며, 건강한 성인에게는 적당한 양이 안전하지만 과다 섭취는 부정적 영향을 줄 수 있습니다.

text:
Conversely, overuse of caffeine-containing medications
may expose patients to overuse headache and lead to the development of a chronic form
of tension headache or chronic migraine. The important role of caffeine in the treatment
of certain types of headaches should also be emphasized: hypnic headache, post-dural
puncture headache and spontaneous intracranial hypotension. At the same time, the results
of an uncontrolled clinical trial suggest that stopping caffeine consumption may improve
the effectiveness of treatment of acute migraine [42].
Headache patients may be advised to continue drinking caffeinated beverages as
long as they consume low to moderate amounts of caffeine (preferably <200 mg/day)
and their daily caffeine intake is similar. If patients cannot consume a similar dose of
caffeine throughout the day and maintain a regular level, the best solution is to gradually
withdraw from caffeine. Patients should also be advised that the interval between doses
of caffeine-containing beverages should not exceed 24 h, as a longer interval may lead to
caffeine-withdrawal headache. Patients should also be informed that daily doses of caffeine
in excess of 200 mg/day may provoke headaches in some patients [79].
There are other food elements that are used as “medicines” or used complementarily to
relieve the ailments and symptoms of various diseases or in any case to treat disorders. The
molecular pathways and targets of drugs and nutrients are closely related. Food contains
compounds, mostly produced by microorganisms or higher plants, that are capable of
producing biological effects that go far beyond nutrition. Many classic drugs are derived
from natural compounds [80]. Currently, more and more attention is paid to nutraceuticals,
i.e., products that, apart from nutrition, also have medicinal applications. Nutraceuticals
can be used to improve health, delay the aging process, prevent chronic disease, increase
life expectancy or support the structure and function of the body. Caffeine is considered a
nutraceutical, which like other herbal stimulants such as ephedrine, chitosan, mahuang
guarana and green tea, are effective in facilitating weight loss in obese people. However,
their use is controversial due to potential side effects. Some popular nutraceuticals include
ginseng, Echinacea, green tea, glucosamine, folic acid and cod liver oil. Flavonoids are
widely distributed in vegetables, onions, chicory, grapefruit, apples, cherries, pomegranates,
blueberries and red wine and play a major role in the prevention and treatment of car-
diovascular disease. The potential role of nutraceuticals is indicated in the treatment of
difﬁcult disorders associated with oxidative stress, including allergies, Alzheimer’s disease,

[Page 13]
Nutrients 2023, 15, 3170 13 of 16
cardiovascular disease, cancer, diabetes, eye diseases, immune disorders, inﬂammatory
diseases and Parkinson’s disease, as well as obesity [81].
Knowledge about the probable, though not fully understood, mechanisms of action
of caffeine in headaches and awareness of its role in the treatment of headache patients
should be disseminated and contribute to optimal use of this substance. It can help people
avoid headaches, limit their triggering and be used to treat certain types of headaches. In
clinical practice, certain groups of patients may beneﬁt from the use of caffeine-containing
medicines, especially patients with a partial response to simple analgesics and migraine
patients with severe nausea associated with gastroparesis. Caffeine is of great importance
(as an adjuvant in ﬁxed combinations with analgesics) in the acute management of tension
headaches and migraines [42].
The role of caffeine and its safety in medicine require further research. Therefore,
caution should be exercised in its use until existing clinical observations are supported by
scientiﬁc evidence.
Author Contributions: A.Z. is the ﬁrst author, came up with the topic, collected basic data from
the literature and designed the basis of the article; J.C., S.Z. and I.D. are equal co-authors, collected
supplementary literature and analyzed data from available publications; all authors (A.Z., J.C., S.Z.
and I.D.) were equally involved in the ﬁnal design and writing of the article. All authors have read
and agreed to the published version of the manuscript.

proﬁt sectors.
Institutional Review Board Statement: Not applicable.
Informed Consent Statement: Not applicable.
Data Availability Statement: Not applicable; the article is a review of previously published research.
Conﬂicts of Interest: All authors declare no conﬂict of interest.

metadata.core_insight_en:
Caffeine affects headaches through various mechanisms, and its consumption can both relieve and trigger headaches. 
                 It can also cause caffeine-withdrawal headache when abruptly discontinued. 
                 Moderate amounts of caffeine are safe for healthy adults, but high doses can have negative effects.

text:
[Page 1]
Applied nutritional investigation
Effects of caffeine on cerebral blood ﬂow
Catarina Gaspar BSc a, Clara Rocha PhD a,b,c,*, Jorge Balteiro MSc a, Helder Santos PhD a
a Polytechnic Institute of Coimbra, Coimbra Health School, Rua 5 de Outubro /C0S. Martinho do Bispo, Apartado 7006, 3046-854 Coimbra, Portugal
b INESC Coimbra, Department of Electrical and Computer Engineering, Polo 2, 3030-290 Coimbra, Portugal
c Laboratory of Applied Health Research (LabinSa /C19ude), Polytechnic Institute of Coimbra, Coimbra, Portugal
ARTICLE INFO
Article History:
Received 24 May 2023
Received in revised form 23 August 2023
Accepted 1 September 2023
ABSTRACT
Objective: The objective of the present study is to evaluate whether, after caffeine ingestion, there are varia-
tions in blood velocity of the middle cerebral arteries in clinically healthy young people as well as to evaluate
whether this variation is dependent on the administered dose.
Methods: We used transcranial Doppler ultrasonography to record blood velocities of the middle cerebral
arteries in three groups of 15 clinically healthy young adults each: no caffeine, a45 mg, and 120 mg of caf-
feine groups. Transcranial Doppler ultrasonography provided simultaneous bilateral velocity of the middle
cerebral arteries measurements while participants performed functional tests (hyperventilation and hypo-
ventilation orders) and three cognitive activities (test 1, short-term memory; test 2, solving a vocabulary
problem; and test 3, solving a math problem) each in 31-s tests with 1-min rests between them. Participants
were assessed before and 30 min after caffeine ingestion.
Results: There was a signi ﬁcant decrease in mean velocity, peak systolic velocity, end-diastolic velocity, and
heart rate after high caffeine intake, except in hyperventilation, which was only observed in peak systolic
velocity. With the intake of a lower dose, signi ﬁcant decreases were seen with hypoventilation and with test
1. In hyperventilation, there was only a signi ﬁcant decrease in end-diastolic velocity and heart rate; in test 2,
it was found in mean velocity and peak systolic velocity; and in test 3, only in heart rate.
Conclusion: With this study, we conclude that caffeine in ﬂuences the cardiovascular system acutely,
interfering with the velocity of the middle cerebra l arteries, causing its decrease. We also conclude
that this acute effect causes vasodilation of the cer ebral arteries, more acce ntuated with higher doses
of caffeine.
© 2023 The Author(s). Published by Elsevier Inc. T his is an open access article under the CC BY-NC
license ( http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc/4.0/ )
Keywords:
Transcranial Doppler ultrasonography
Middle cerebral artery
Functional tests
Cognitive activities
Caffeine
Introduction
Caffeine is a sympathomimetic agent and adenosine antagonist,
which is present in coffee, tea, and soft drinks; chocolates; and
energy drinks; it is the most widely used pharmacologically active
substance in the world. Caffeine exposure is generally long lasting,
and the prevalence of exposure is very high in most countries.
Moderate doses of caffeine are considered to have an effect similar
to a mild central nervous system stimulant and, therefore, are
taken to increase alertness, to provide cognitive and humoral
enhancement, and also to provide boosts of extra energy. However,
in high doses, it can cause adverse effects, such as intoxication
[1/C07]. Regular caffeine consumption, 2 to 4 cups of coffee/d, elimi-
nates the protective effect of ischemic preconditioning [8] and
reduces global cerebral blood ﬂow by »22% to 30% [ 9,10]. Long-
term caffeine use increases blood pressure [ 4,11,12], increases vas-
cular resistance [13], and causes arterial stiffness [5] and cerebral
vasoconstriction [14].
Although caffeine reduces regional cerebral blood ﬂow [8], the
effects of caffeine on cerebral blood ﬂow velocity are largely
undocumented. Couturier et al. [15] found that ﬂow velocity in the
middle cerebral artery (VMCA), posterior cerebral artery, and basi-
lar artery slowed down by 9%, 4%, and 3% respectively, 30 min after
The research project was accepted by the Ethics Committee of the Polytechnic
Institute of Coimbra (no. 91 CEPC2/2021) and was performed in accordance with
the ethical standards and principles referred to in the 1964 Declaration of Helsinki
and its later amendments. Con ﬁdentiality and anonymity of the data collected were
ensured, as they were collected for scienti ﬁc purposes, without any cost or compen-
sation to the participants.
Written informed consent was obtained from all participants who participated in
this study.
The study was retrospectively registered at ClinicalTrials.gov (ID: NCT05866848)
on 19-05-2023.
*Corresponding author: Tel.: +351 239 802 430
E-mail address: clarapr@estesc.ipc.pt (C. Rocha).
https://doi.org/10.1016/j.nut.2023.112217
0899-9007/© 2023 The Author(s). Published by Elsevier Inc. This is an open access article under the CC BY-NC license ( http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc/4.0/)
Nutrition 117 (2024) 112217
Contents lists available at ScienceDirect
Nutrition
journal homepage: www.nutritionjrnl.com


[Page 2]
the intake of 150 mg of caffeine. They used transcranial Doppler
ultrasonography to access cerebral blood ﬂow velocity.
Caffeine is transported through the blood to the central nervous
system, after its absorption in the stomach and small intestine. In
this aspect, caffeine and adenosine have great similarities in their
chemical structures. For this reason, caffeine binds to adenosine A1
and A2 receptors, blocking them. Caffeine can negatively affect the
action of adenosine, known to reduce the release of neurotransmit-
ters, such as acetylcholine, dopamine, noradrenaline, g-aminobu-
tyric acid, and serotonin, all of which improve the mood, stimulate
the body, improve concentration, and eliminate physical fatigue.
Caffeine increases the concentration of these neurotransmitters in
the body [5]. Caffeine also inhibits the activity of phosphodiester-
ase, an enzyme responsible for the degradation of cyclic adenosine
monophosphate (cAMP). Effects of caffeine on the blood ﬂow
velocities of the cerebral arteries non-cyclic form 5 /C0adenosine
monophosphate. Inhibition of phosphodiesterase increases the
concentration of cAMP in cells and also raises blood pressure
[2/C04,6,7].
Ingested or administered, caffeine diffuses throughout the body
with a volume of distribution similar to that of a body of water
[16] and rapidly penetrates the brain [17]. Because it is highly solu-
ble in lipids, it quickly crosses the blood-brain barrier both by dif-
fusion and by a saturable transport system [18].

metadata.core_insight_en: This study investigates the effects of caffeine on cerebral blood flow in healthy young adults. The researchers used transcranial Doppler ultrasonography to measure blood velocities in the middle cerebral arteries before and after caffeine ingestion. The results show that high doses of caffeine decrease blood velocity, heart rate, and cause vasodilation. Lower doses also decrease blood velocity, but to a lesser extent.
metadata.core_insight_ko: 이 연구는 건강한 젊은 성인에서 카페인이 뇌 혈류에 미치는 영향을 조사했습니다. 연구자들은 경두동맥 초음파를 이용해 카페인 섭취 전후 중간 대뇌동맥의 혈류 속도를 측정했으며, 결과적으로 고농도 카페인은 혈류 속도, 심박수를 감소시키고 혈관 확장을 유발하는 것으로 나타났습니다. 저농도 카페인은 혈류 속도를 감소시키지만 그 효과는 덜했습니다.

text:
Caffeine increases the concentration of these neurotransmitters in
the body [5]. Caffeine also inhibits the activity of phosphodiester-
ase, an enzyme responsible for the degradation of cyclic adenosine
monophosphate (cAMP). Effects of caffeine on the blood ﬂow
velocities of the cerebral arteries non-cyclic form 5 /C0adenosine
monophosphate. Inhibition of phosphodiesterase increases the
concentration of cAMP in cells and also raises blood pressure
[2/C04,6,7].
Ingested or administered, caffeine diffuses throughout the body
with a volume of distribution similar to that of a body of water
[16] and rapidly penetrates the brain [17]. Because it is highly solu-
ble in lipids, it quickly crosses the blood-brain barrier both by dif-
fusion and by a saturable transport system [18]. Some animal
experiments have found that the caffeine concentration in the
cerebrospinal ﬂuid reaches half its plasma level in just 4 to 8 min
[19], and that the average concentration in the brain remains sta-
ble for /C21 1h [20]. According to Kaplan et al. [21] study, mean
plasma and brain caffeine concentrations are proportional to the
dose of caffeine administered 1 h before. The authors concluded
that the concentrations mentioned above and their metabolites
can be an accurate indicator of the concentration of these substan-
ces in the brain, both in animals and in humans. Hypoventilation is
a decrease in alveolar ventilation. With this reduction in ventila-
tion, the elimination of carbon dioxide (CO 2) also decreases, which
causes its concentration to increase in the blood (hypercapnia),
lowering the blood pH. Hypercapnia occurs in association with
hypoxia when these are caused by hypoventilation. In hypoxia
caused by hypoventilation, the CO 2 transfer is as affected as the
oxygen transfer, with hypercapnia occurring in conjunction with
hypoxia. In circulatory de ﬁciency, decreased blood ﬂow reduces
the removal of CO 2 from tissues, resulting in hypercapnia tissue, in
addition to hypoxia tissue. However, the transport capacity of
blood for CO 2 is three times greater than that of oxygen, making
the resulting hypercapnia tissue much smaller than the hypoxia
tissue [22].When partial pressure of CO 2 (PCO2) rises above 60 to
75 mm Hg, a normal healthy person is dyspneic, breathing as
quickly and deeply as possible. If this increases to 80 to 100 mm
Hg, the individual becomes lethargic and sometimes semi-coma-
tose. Death can occur when Pco 2 PCO2 reaches 120 to 150 mm Hg
[23,24].
When high levels of Pco 2 are reached, excess CO 2 begins to
depress respiration instead of stimulating it, which creates a
vicious cycle: CO 2 increases and respiration decreases, causing
more CO2 to accumulate, quickly culminating in respiratory death.
Given that caffeine can suppress baseline velocities [15], its
presence may interact in one of two ways with tests designed to
induce changes in cerebrovascular reactivity, such as hyperventila-
tion and hypoventilation requests or cognitive activities.
During hyperventilation, PCO 2 levels may decrease to values
capable of causing peripheral vasodilation and lowering blood
pressure. Dizziness due to a decrease in the supply of oxygen to
the brain may occur, caused by reduced cerebral blood ﬂow, which
in turn is a result of the drop in blood pressure. The reduction in
global cerebral blood ﬂow in response to hypocapnia results from
cerebral vasoconstriction, attributed exclusively to arterial partial
pressure of CO 2 (PaCO2) and directly related to changes in the pH of
the cerebrospinal ﬂuid [25].
A decrease in P CO2 can result in a reduction in the VMCA, fol-
lowed by a slow and progressive rise, probably caused by meta-
bolic compensation of pH changes.
In humans, some studies have been consistent in describing a
linear response of cerebral ﬂow with partial pressure of end-tidal
carbon dioxide (PET CO2) in both hypocapnia and hypercapnia,
whereas other studies have not; these have suggested that there is
an exponential or sigmoidal relationship. Although cerebral blood
ﬂow is reduced in acute hypocapnia, it returns to normal values
within minutes regardless of whether Pa CO2 or PET CO2 values
remain low. Studies have also reported the presence of an under-
shoot or overshoot in cerebral ﬂow when PET CO2 returns to
eucapnic values, after a period of sustained hypercapnia or hypo-
capnia, respectively. The reason for this is still unclear, but it is
believed that it may re ﬂect the change or adaptation of the mecha-
nisms responsible for vasodilation and vasoconstriction of cerebral
vessels, caused by hypercapnia and hypocapnia, respectively
[23,24].
Transcranial Doppler ultrasonography was introduced in
behavioral studies by Droste et al. [ 26,27]. It ﬁnds that the speed of
brain ﬂow is higher when in participants during in cognitive activi-
ties compared with periods of rest that do not involve cognitive
activity. Varnadore et al. [11] have reported that middle cerebral
artery (MCA) speed increases bilaterally when participants listen
to music, associate words, perform memory exercises, or perform
problem solving.
Aaslid [28] found an increase in VMCA of 3.3% after light stimu-
lation, more prominent during the ﬁrst 8 s after the end of stimula-
tion and with adaptation after 10 to 15 s. No statistically signi ﬁcant
differences were found between left and right VMCA, both at base-
line and during exercises, either between left-and right-handed
participants or between women and men. In the elderly, the mean
values of reference at rest are lower in the left and right VMCAs
than in young people.
The aim of the present study is to assess whether, after caffeine
ingestion, there is variation in VMCA measurements in clinically
healthy young people as well as whether this variation is depen-
dent on the administered dose. We used transcranial Doppler
ultrasonography to record VMCA measurements in three groups of
participants: no-, lower-, and higher-caffeine groups, while per-
forming functional tests (hyperventilation and hypoventilation
requests) and cognitive activities.
Materials and methods
A randomized controlled trial was performed in accordance with the ethical
standards and principles referred to in the 1964 Declaration of Helsinki and its
later amendments. It was registered in the ClinicalTrials.gov database (Identi ﬁer:
NCT05866848). All participants were informed and clari ﬁed about the study pro-
cedures and signed an informed consent document describing the objectives and
work methods. The research project was accepted by the Ethics Committee of the
Polytechnic Institute of Coimbra (no. 91 CEPC2/2021. Con ﬁdentiality and anonym-
ity of the data collected were ensured, as they were collected for scienti ﬁc pur-
poses, without any cost or compensation to the participants.

The researchers used transcranial Doppler ultrasonography to measure blood velocities in the middle cerebral arteries before and after caffeine ingestion. The results show that high doses of caffeine decrease blood velocity, heart rate, and cause vasodilation. Lower doses also decrease blood velocity, but to a lesser extent.
metadata.core_insight_ko: 이 연구는 건강한 젊은 성인에서 카페인이 뇌 혈류에 미치는 영향을 조사했습니다. 연구자들은 경두동맥 초음파를 이용해 카페인 섭취 전후 중동동맥의 혈류 속도를 측정했으며, 결과적으로 고농도 카페인은 혈류 속도와 심박수를 감소시키고 혈관 확장을 유발하는 것으로 나타났습니다. 반면 저농도 카페인은 혈류 속도를 감소시키지만 그 효과는 덜 두드러졌습니다.

text:
Materials and methods
A randomized controlled trial was performed in accordance with the ethical
standards and principles referred to in the 1964 Declaration of Helsinki and its
later amendments. It was registered in the ClinicalTrials.gov database (Identi ﬁer:
NCT05866848). All participants were informed and clari ﬁed about the study pro-
cedures and signed an informed consent document describing the objectives and
work methods. The research project was accepted by the Ethics Committee of the
Polytechnic Institute of Coimbra (no. 91 CEPC2/2021. Con ﬁdentiality and anonym-
ity of the data collected were ensured, as they were collected for scienti ﬁc pur-
poses, without any cost or compensation to the participants.
Participants
The sample consisted of 45 clinically healthy university students, from a
school of health technology, ages between 18 and 22 y. Participants in the study
were recruited by a non-probabilistic sample, including all the students, who vol-
untarily agreed to participate in the study. Individuals with previously known
hypertension, cholesterol, diabetes, arrhythmias, carotid sinus syndrome, or
severe carotid stenosis or who were in an unstable clinical situation did not partic-
ipate in the study.
2 C. Gaspar et al. / Nutrition 117 (2024) 112217

[Page 3]
Study participants were not regular coffee consumers, 21 of them did not con-
sume it at all, 16 consumed /C20 1 coffee/d, and 8 consumed /C20 2 coffees/d, with
a majority consuming only 1 or 2 coffees/day in abnormal situations, such as exam
season.
Instrumentation
In this study, capsules dosed with 45 and 120 mg of anhydrous caffeine and
ﬂour capsules were used as placebo using a semiautomatic capper no. 0 (Capsu-
norm; TecnyFarma), hard gelatin capsules no. 0 by (Acofarma), and caffeine anhy-
drous capsules (Chemical Abstracts Service no. 58-08-2; Biochem) in its
preparation. We used the Sonara (Viasys) transcranial Doppler unit and a 2-MHz
probe to sample the MCA at depths of 60 mm, using emission Doppler pulsed per-
forming spectral analysis in real time. Two probes (bilaterally) of 2 MHz were
placed at a depth between 55 and 60 mm, thus making it possible to analyze both
MCAs simultaneously. During the evaluation, there were no signi ﬁcant differences
between the left and right MCA, so we chose to use the values of the right MCA for
practical reasons.
Study protocol
Three groups were randomly constituted, with 15 young people each: control
group without caffeine, group with 45 mg of caffeine administered, and group
with 120 mg administered. All of these were submitted to two points of evalua-
tion: the ﬁrst, at baseline, after the participants arrived in the room and rested for
5 min to eliminate any effects caused by the walk to the room (T0); and the sec-
ond, 30 min after taking the caffeine (T1), which is the necessary time for the
absorption of the caffeine [ 1,15].
Each participant was asked not to ingest caffeine and exercise vigorously for
/C21 12 h before the assessment. In cases of smokers, they were also asked not to
smoke for /C21 2 h before the evaluation. The laboratory had medium luminosity, was
silent, and had controlled temperature (23°C/24°C). To avoid interobserver errors,
all examinations were performed by the same investigator who was not aware of
what dose had been administered to the participant.
Participants were tested while seated in a reclining chair located in a dimly lit,
sound-attenuated room and performed the activities with their eyes closed. Partic-
ipants ﬁrst performed 30 s of continuous breathing (hypoventilation) followed by
30 s of continuous rapid breathing (hyperventilation). The VMCA measures were
allowed to stabilize between these tests. We gave the breath-holding test ﬁrst,
because VMCA requires less time to return to baseline levels following hypoventi-
lation than after hyperventilation.
When VMCA measures were stable, a 30-s baseline period was followed by
three 30-s cognitive tasks, with a 1-min pause between activities: test 1, short-
term memory (recalling letters and numbers in the order given (e.g., 22, A, 3, IG,
and 4)); test 2, solving a vocabulary problem (making new words from the letters
of a target word, e.g., mother); and test 3, solving a math problem (e.g., “With a
group of four people, how many different subgroups with two elements each can
we form? ”). Three versions of each cognitive task were used for the three condi-
tions, and the sequence of tasks was randomized within and between participants.
Each task stimulus was read to the participant, the participant performed the
activity silently, and answers were obtained at the end of the cognitive period.
Measures were not collected during the instructions or when the answers were
given. The procedure concluded with a ﬁnal 62-s rest period. Each session required
(on average) 30 min. At all six evaluation points, the mean velocity (Vm), peak sys-
tolic velocity (Vp), end-diastolic velocity (Vend), and heart rate (HR) were
recorded.
Statistical analysis
Categorical variables were described by frequency and percentage and contin-
uous variables by mean and SD. To evaluate the normality of distribution, the Sha-
piro-Wilk test was used. Comparisons between the two evaluation points, T0 and
T1, were made using the Student ’s t test paired samples, in case of normality of
the variable, or according to the non-parametric Wilcoxon test, if normality is not
veriﬁed. The level of statistical signi ﬁcance used was P < 0.05. Statistical analysis
was performed using software SPSS version 27 IBM Corp., Armonk, NY, USA).
Results
Forty-ﬁve healthy young people, including 39 women (86.7%),
ages between 18 and 22 y, were recruited and had no previously
known high blood pressure, diabetes or cholesterol, or any per-
sonal cardiovascular history.
In the control group without caffeine, ﬁve of the participants
did not consume coffee (33.3%), six consumed /C20 1 coffee/d (40%),
and four consumed /C20 2 coffees/d (26.7%). In the group with a 45-
mg amount of caffeine administered, eight of the participants did
not consume coffee (53.3%), four consumed /C20 1 coffee/d (26.7%),
and three consumed /C20 2 coffees/d (20%). In the group with a
greater amount of caffeine (120 mg), eight of the participants did
not consume coffee (53.3%), six consumed /C20 1 coffee/d (40%), and
one consumed /C20 2 coffees/d (6.7%). It was also mentioned by all
young people in the study that maximum coffee consumption is
usually used in times of stress, such as exam seasons, and data col-
lection did not include any of the mentioned phases.

Table 1 lists the mean values and SD of each variable analyzed
in the sample as well as their signi ﬁcance values during the evalua-
tion.
Effects of placebo
In the control group participants, there were no signi ﬁcant
changes from T0 to T1 in the values of Vm, Vp,and Vend, but delta
(Δ) variations of the mean were higher for HR in baseline assess-
ment ( Δ = /C07.5%; P = 0.007), test 1 ( Δ = /C09.2%; P = 0.011), test 2
(Δ = /C08.6%; P = 0.004), and test 3 ( Δ = /C010.7%; P = 0.002) (Fig. 1A).
Effects of 45 mg of caffeine
In the group who ingested a dose of 45 mg of caffeine, statistically
signiﬁcant decreases are found from T0 to T1 in Vm of hypoventilation
(Δ = /C011.1%; P = 0.003), test 1 ( Δ = /C013.7%; P =0 . 0 1 ) ,a n dt e s t2
(Δ = /C012.1%; P = 0.045); in Vp of hypoventilation (Δ = /C08.6%; P =0 . 0 4 ) ,
test 1 ( Δ = /C010.4%; P = 0.023), and test 2 ( Δ = /C08.8%; P = 0.046); in
Vend of hypoventilation ( Δ = /C014.1%; P = 0.004), hyperventilation
(Δ = /C014.8%; P = 0.026), and test 1 (Δ = /C015.8%; P = 0.009); and in HR
of hypoventilation (Δ = /C09.5%; P = 0.021), hyperventilation (Δ = /C09.2%;
P = 0.019), test 1 ( Δ = /C012.2%; P < 0.001), and test 3 ( Δ = /C08.0%;
P = 0.007). In the other parameters, the differences were not statisti-
cally signi ﬁcant. Comparing before and after doses, there was a
decrease in all studied variables (Vm, Vp, Vend, and HR) in all evalua-
tions performed (Fig. 1B).
Effects of 120 mg of caffeine amount
In the group who ingested a dose of 120 mg of caffeine, statistically
signiﬁcant decreases are found from T0 to T1 in all parameters: in Vm
of basal ( Δ = /C016.9%; P = 0.027), hypoventilation ( Δ = /C022.1%;
P = 0.001), test 1 (Δ = /C017.1%; P =0 . 0 1 5 ) ,t e s t2(Δ = /C016.4%; P = 0.013),
and test 3 (Δ = /C016.3%;P =0 . 0 1 ) ;i nV po fb a s a l(Δ = /C016.4%;P = 0.014),
hypoventilation ( Δ = /C021.5%; P < 0.001), hyperventilation
(Δ = /C015.9%; P = 0.013), test 1 ( Δ = /C016.8%; P = 0.013), test 2
(Δ = /C017.2%; P =0 . 0 1 ) ,a n dt e s t3(Δ = /C016.1%; P = 0.015); in Vend of
basal (Δ = /C019.2%; P = 0.042), hypoventilation (Δ = /C024.7%; P = 0.014),
test 1 (Δ = /C018.5%; P = 0.023), test 2 (Δ = /C017.9%; P =0 . 0 2 2 ) ,a n dt e s t3
(Δ = /C015.9%; P = 0.021); and in HR of basal ( Δ = /C09.5%; P = 0.006),
hypoventilation (Δ = /C013.7%; P = 0.008), test 1 ( Δ = /C07.8%; P = 0.01),
test 2 (Δ = /C09.9%; P =0 . 0 0 3 ) ,a n dt e s t3(Δ = /C05.6%; P = 0.011). In the
other parameters, the differences were not statistically signi ﬁcant
(Fig. 1C).
Changes during functional tests
At the second evaluation point, T1, we veri ﬁed that at the end of
the hypoventilation, the velocities had a signi ﬁcant increase in
comparison with the baseline values, both in the control group and
in the groups who ingested 45 and 120 mg of caffeine: Vm
(Δ = 16.9%, P = 0.025, Δ = 14.4%, P = 0.001, and Δ = 19.3%, P = 0.001;
respectively), Vp ( Δ = 11.6%, P = 0.003; Δ = 10.5%, P = 0.001; and
Δ = 9.3%, P = 0.001; respectively), and Vend ( Δ = 25.8%; P = 0.015;
C. Gaspar et al. / Nutrition 117 (2024) 112217 3

[Page 4]
Δ = 18.9%; P = 0.002; and Δ = 22.7%, P = 0.013; respectively). We also
veriﬁed a signi ﬁcant decrease in HR values in the group who
ingested 45 mg of caffeine ( Δ = /C08.7%; P = 0.011) and the group
who ingested a 120 mg of caffeine ( Δ = /C09.4%; P = 0.002) (Fig. 2A).

[Page 4]
Δ = 18.9%; P = 0.002; and Δ = 22.7%, P = 0.013; respectively). We also
veriﬁed a signi ﬁcant decrease in HR values in the group who
ingested 45 mg of caffeine ( Δ = /C08.7%; P = 0.011) and the group
who ingested a 120 mg of caffeine ( Δ = /C09.4%; P = 0.002) (Fig. 2A).
Regarding hyperventilation, there was a high decrease in veloc-
ity values in each of the groups compared with baseline values:
Vm (Δ = /C031.8%, P < 0.001; Δ = /C024.7%, P < 0.001; and Δ = /C020.2%,
P < 0.001; respectively), Vp ( Δ = /C016.2%, P = 0.028; Δ = /C06.9%,
P = 0.078; and Δ = /C08.0%, P < 0.001; respectively), and Vend
(Δ = /C044.0%, P < 0.001; Δ = /C036.3%, P < 0.001; and Δ = /C031.3%, P <
0.001; respectively). We also veri ﬁed a signi ﬁcant increase in HR
values in the control group ( Δ = 21.9%; P < 0.001), the group who
ingested 45 mg of caffeine ( Δ = 17.8%; P = 0.003), and the group
who ingested a 120 mg of caffeine ( Δ = 19.7%; P = 0.002) (Fig. 2A).
Changes during cognitive activities
In the present study, the cognitive tests caused a slight increase
in brain speeds of the participants in the ﬁrst contact with the test,
and after getting the answer, they quickly returned to values close
to the baseline. This increase was only signi ﬁcant in control group
at the end of test 3 in Vend values ( Δ = 8.9%; P = 0.041) and in the
group who took 120 mg of caffeine and at the end of test 1 in Vend
values (Δ = 6.2%; P = 0.047) and HR ( Δ = 8.6%; P = 0.024) (Fig. 2B).
Discussion
Caffeine is the most administered pharmacologically active
agent in the world. It is known as a central nervous system
stimulant that helps increase alertness, decrease fatigue, and
improve mood.
The aim of the present study was to evaluate whether caffeine
intake leads to variation in VMCAs in clinically healthy young peo-
ple as well as whether the variation is dependent on the adminis-
tered dose. Different doses of caffeine were administered to three
different groups of young people: no caffeine, 45 mg of caffeine,
and 120 mg of caffeine.
Our results ﬁnd that, at baseline, no caffeine and 45 mg of caf-
feine do not affect the VMCA. However, 120 mg of caffeine led to a
signiﬁcant drop of 16.9% in Vm, 16.4% in Vp, and 19.2% in Vend.
Regarding HR, there was a signi ﬁcant decrease of 7.5% in the pla-
cebo group and 9.5% in the group who took 120 mg of caffeine,
possibly caused by the 30-min rest.
During hypoventilation, we found that the velocities
decreased slightly in the ﬁrst 10 s and in the remaining 20 s they
progressively increased, ending up with values higher than those
obtained in the baseline evaluation. Caffeine ingestion led to a
signiﬁcant decrease in velocities (24.7% in the group who
ingested 120 mg and 21.5% in the group who ingested 45 mg) as
well as in HR (13.7%). On the other hand, there were no changes
in the placebo group. Lassen [24] described hypoventilation as
t h ec a u s eo ft h em e a ni n c r e a s ei na r t e r i a lC O2 pressure from 43
to 52 mm Hg, causing hypercapnia that may be important to
maintain cerebral ﬂow at higher values. In his work, cerebral
ﬂow values also slightly increased compared with control values,
without the effects of any pharmacologically active substance.
Another study [1] observed, during hypoventilation, an initial
temporary acceleration of the VMCA followed by a drop to values

The researchers used transcranial Doppler ultrasonography to measure blood velocities in the middle cerebral arteries before and after caffeine ingestion. The results show that high doses of caffeine decrease blood velocity, heart rate, and cause vasodilation. Lower doses also decrease blood velocity, but to a lesser extent.

ation and ending, as in our own study, with values signi ﬁcantly
higher than baseline, with a difference between 17% and 23%.
Contrary to what was veri ﬁed in our study, no in ﬂuence of the
administered dose was found.
Valdueza et al. [13] performed an assessment of variations in
cerebral ﬂow velocity during hyperventilation without the

(Hiper), and cognitive activities (test 1, test 2, and test 3), in control (A), 45 mg (B), and 120 mg (C), caffeine amount groups. * P < 0.05; **P < 0.01; ***P < 0.001. HR, heart rate;

C. Gaspar et al. / Nutrition 117 (2024) 112217 5

[Page 6]
inﬂuence of pharmacological agents, using transcranial Doppler
and magnetic resonance imaging. Baseline VMCA values ranged
from 62 to 100 cm/s (73.7 § 13.3 cm/s); hyperventilation led to a
rapid reduction in maximum velocity after 10 to 20 s from the
beginning of the maneuver until reaching a minimum plateau of
the maximum VMCA, that remained unchanged until the end of
hyperventilation. They also found a signi ﬁcant decline in Pa
CO2,
which, because of the hypocapnia caused by hyperventilation, is to
be expected. The percentage of fall in V p had a signi ﬁcance of P <
0.001; the values achieved ranged between 29 and 60 cm/s (37.6 §
10.6 cm/s), with the decrease oscillating between 40% and 60%
(49.6 § 5.7) of baseline V p.
Perod et al. [1] veriﬁed that the lowest VMCA during hyperven-
tilation was reached 10 to 12 s after its beginning in the three
groups evaluated in their study (a control group and two groups
with an intake of 45 mg and 117 mg of caffeine, obtained by drink-
ing a Coke and a coffee, respectively). VMCA remained low during
the remaining maneuver in control participants, whereas in caf-
feine ingestion groups, it became progressively less suppressed.
After 18 s of the maneuver, the velocities, in both caffeine condi-
tions, did not decrease as much as in the control group and, in the
last 9 s of the maneuver, they did not differ from baseline values.
During our investigation, it was observed that in hyperventilation,
from its onset, the velocities decrease substantially, reaching a
minimum of 15 to 20 s after the beginning, maintaining these val-
ues until the end of 30 s. The greatest decrease in velocity was the
Vend because of cerebral vasoconstriction caused by the Pa CO2
deﬁcit. In this evaluation, after caffeine intake, there was a signi ﬁ-
cant decrease in Vm ( Δ between /C020.2% and /C031.8%), Vp ( Δ
between /C06.9% and /C016.2%), and Vend ( Δ between /C031.3% and
/C044.0%) velocities in relation to post-dose baseline values, being
greater in the control group, followed by the group who ingested a
45 mg of caffeine. On the other hand, HR suffered a signi ﬁcant
increase when compared with the baseline value, with Δ between
17.8% and /C021.9%.
Perod et al. [1] veriﬁed that during the four cognitive tests,
although there were no signi ﬁcant differences between the test,
they produced a global speed greater than the respective baseline
speeds, regardless of caffeine intake or not. The decrease in the
VMCA in the remaining phases in the group who ingested the 117-
mg dose, compared with the other two groups, was maintained
during the cognitive tests. Harders et al. [14] veriﬁed that, in all
cognitive tasks performed, a maximum speed was reached »8s
after the task started. This was followed by a decrease to the indi-
vidual’s baseline value. When the tasks were ﬁnished, the veloci-
ties remained similar to the baseline value for »9 s, followed by a
decrease with an undershoot (below baseline), with a normaliza-
tion in /C20 33 s.
In the present study, the cognitive tests caused in the partici-
pants a slight increase in brain speeds at the ﬁrst contact with the
test, and, after achieving the response, they quickly return to val-
ues close to baseline. This initial increase was veri ﬁed in all groups
regardless of the dose of caffeine ingested by the participant. It
was also possible to verify that, in the placebo group, regarding the
Fig. 2. Percentage of delta (% D): the difference baseline /C0post caffeine for Vm, Vp, Vend, and HR in control, smaller, and larger caffeine amounts, in functional tests (A) in and
cognitive activities (B). * P < 0.05; **P < 0.01; ***P < 0.001. HR, heart rate; Vend, end-diastolic velocity; Vm, mean velocity; VP, peak systolic velocity.
6 C. Gaspar et al. / Nutrition 117 (2024) 112217

[Page 7]
pre/C0post caffeine drinking variations, there were statistically sig-
niﬁcant variations only in HR (between 8.6% and 10.7%), which can
be explained by the fact that the participant was calm and at rest
for 30 min. In the group who ingested 45 mg of caffeine, there was
a decrease in speed between 6.2% and 14%. In the group who
ingested 120 mg, there was a decrease between 15.9% and 18.5%,
and the HR decreased between 5.6% and 12.2%.
Mathew et al. [8] conﬁrmed that caffeine reduces cerebral ﬂow
after ingestion, which goes against what was said by Gibbs et al. in
1935 [ 29] and by Shenkin in 1951 [30]. Furthermore, their results
indicated that the ﬂow decreases do not have regional differences,
the maximum change was with 250 mg of caffeine, and the incre-
ment for higher doses did not cause cerebral blood ﬂow to
decrease further.

metadata.core_insight_en: This study investigates the effects of caffeine on cerebral blood flow in healthy young adults. The researchers used transcranial Doppler ultrasonography to measure blood velocities in the middle cerebral arteries before and after caffeine ingestion. The results show that high doses of caffeine decrease blood velocity, heart rate, and cause vasodilation. Lower doses also decrease blood velocity, but to a lesser extent.
metadata.core_insight_ko: 본 연구는 건강한 젊은 성인들을 대상으로 카페인이 뇌 혈류에 미치는 영향을 조사했습니다. 연구자들은 경두동맥 초음파를 이용해 카페인 섭취 전후 중동동맥의 혈류 속도를 측정했으며, 결과적으로 고농도 카페인은 혈류 속도와 심박수를 감소시키고 혈관 확장을 유발하는 것으로 나타났습니다. 저농도 카페인은 혈류 속도 감소 효과가 덜했습니다.

text:
In the group who ingested 45 mg of caffeine, there was
a decrease in speed between 6.2% and 14%. In the group who
ingested 120 mg, there was a decrease between 15.9% and 18.5%,
and the HR decreased between 5.6% and 12.2%.
Mathew et al. [8] conﬁrmed that caffeine reduces cerebral ﬂow
after ingestion, which goes against what was said by Gibbs et al. in
1935 [ 29] and by Shenkin in 1951 [30]. Furthermore, their results
indicated that the ﬂow decreases do not have regional differences,
the maximum change was with 250 mg of caffeine, and the incre-
ment for higher doses did not cause cerebral blood ﬂow to
decrease further. These results are compatible with those obtained
in the present study, because our most important reductions
occurred at the highest dose (120 mg), whereas at the lowest dose,
there was a drop in speeds not as sharply as the previous one.
Cerebral blood ﬂow measurements mostly include the ﬂow of
the superﬁcial area of the cortex, while, when using the VMCA, it is
possible to assess deeper structures as they are supplied by it [9].
Differences between cerebral ﬂow and VMCA can also be caused
by changes in the speed pro ﬁle, but these changes would cause a
greater change in speed. Therefore, and as in the present study,
there were no changes that might suggest a change in the speed
proﬁle; this factor was not taken into account for justi ﬁcation of
the decrease of the analyzed parameters.
Lunt et al. [9] suggested that differences may occur between the
effect of caffeine on gray and white matter. Obrist and Wilkison
[31] reanalyzed cerebral ﬂow measurements using a dual-slope
model to determine gray matter effects, which had no signi ﬁcant
changes. They found a 24% reduction in ﬂux in gray matter com-
pared with 22% in gray and white matter together. This is consis-
tent with the results obtained by Field et al. [10], who used
magnetic resonance imaging to study the effect of caffeine on gray
matter and white matter ﬂux, having found that a 250-mg dose of
caffeine reduced gray matter ﬂux by 23% and, in white matter, by
18%.
The speci ﬁc mechanisms that alter the speed of cerebral blood
ﬂow during cognitive testing remain unexplored. However,
because of the metabolic needs of brain tissue during its activation
in response to cognitive or functional testing, it is believed that it
may produce changes in diameter from the lumen of arterioles.
These alterations cause changes in the cerebral blood supply,
resulting in a change in velocity. The involvement of these arterio-
les, instead of the large MCA branches, was indicated by the non-
alteration of the lumen in the M1 segment of the MCA during
hyperventilation by Valdueza et al. [13]. With this report, the effect
of caffeine in the MCA, obtained by this study and by others more
commonly used as a bibliographic reference, is paradoxical,
because caffeine works as an arterial cerebral vasoconstrictor. This
means that the velocities should increase and, on the contrary,
decrease.
Conclusions
With this study, it is possible to conclude that, caffeine acutely
inﬂuences the cardiovascular system, interfering with the veloci-
ties of the MCAs. It was found that caffeine intake causes a decrease
in all VMCAs, leading to the conclusion that the acute effect causes
vasodilation of the cerebral arteries. It was also veri ﬁed that the
variation of these speeds is greater with higher doses of caffeine. In
hypoventilation, the reductions found in cerebral hemovelocity
after ingestion of caffeine, in relation to post-dose basal velocities,
are more pronounced with the intake of 120 mg due to more
pronounced vasodilation. Thus, during hyperventilation, it was
found that the drop is more pronounced in the velocities studied
in the placebo group, followed by the 45-mg group. During cogni-
tive activities, there were reduced changes in VMCA.
We also conclude that, in low doses, caffeine has a bene ﬁcial
effect, causing cerebral vasodilation. However, it is important to
carry out further studies to clarify the cumulative in ﬂuence of vari-
ous doses on cerebral hemovelocity and the prolonged effect of
caffeine on VMCDA.
To better understand the effect of caffeine, it will be important
to further analyze VMCA speeds with controlled doses in individu-
als who are not regularly exposed to caffeine and who are frequent
consumers of caffeine (such as coffee, tea, soft drinks, chocolates,
and energy drinks) to try to understand if there is any kind of
habituation and difference between consumers and non-consum-
ers after a session.
As for the limitations of this study, our results come from a sin-
gle dose of caffeine, thus not allowing us to study the cumulative
inﬂuence of several doses on cerebral hemovelocity. Also, the pro-
longed effect of caffeine was not possible to study because several
measurements over time were not carried out.
Declaration of Competing Interest
The authors declare that they have no known competing ﬁnan-
cial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to
inﬂuence the work reported in this paper.
Acknowledgments
The authors would like to thank the participants who agreed to
participate in the study project and the Coimbra Health School for
the availability and provision of equipment, especially Professor
Jorge Conde and Professor Telmo Pereira, for relevant contributions
to this project. They would also like to thank Sara Santos for proof-
reading the English language.

cardiovascular diseases: critical review of current research. Eur J Nutr
2016;55:1331–43
.
[4] Konishi Y, Hori H, Ide K, Katsuki A, Atake K, Igata R, et al. Effect of single caf-

[5] Meeusen R, Roelands B, Spriet LL. Caffeine, exercise and the brain. Nestle Nutr

nisms of action, biochemical, metabolic and psychostimulant effects.
