The questionnaire was reduced to 7 composite variables: Fatigue/drowsiness, Low alertness/difﬁculty concentrating, Mood disturbances, Low socia-bility/motivation to work, Nausea/upset stomach, Flu-like feelings, and Headache. The study found that daily coffee consumers experienced more severe withdrawal symptoms than non-daily consumers.
metadata.core_insight_ko: 연구는 카페인 금단 증상을 측정하기 위한 23개 항목의 카페인 금단 증상 설문지(CWSQ)를 개발했으며, 이를 통해 피로/졸음, 낮은 경각심/집중력 저하, 기분 변화, 사회성 저하/업무 동기 감소, 메스꺼움/소화 불량, 감기 증상 같은 7가지 합성 변수를 도출했습니다. 결과적으로 일일 커피 섭취자가 비일일 섭취자보다 더 심각한 금단 증상을 경험하는 것으로 나타났습니다.

text:
Consistent with prior research, these analyses
demonstrate
 that caffeine abstinence produces subjective effects
that
 are signiﬁcantly more aversive than normal daily experiences.
Although the internal consistency of the nausea/upset stomach
factor
 is at the lower end of what is considered to be acceptable
(Cronbach’s
 alpha = .60), it is not too surprising as this factor con-
sists of only two items. Low internal consistency could explain
why
 the nausea/upset stomach factor was less able to discrimi-
nate between caffeine abstinence and the other conditions. Future
research on the CWSQ should examine additional items pertain-
ing
 to nausea (e.g., “queasy”)  and separate nausea and vomiting (a
combined descriptor in the present study) to potentially increase
the
 stability and sensitivity of this factor. Moreover, it is possible
that
 nausea, vomiting, and upset stomach are more likely to occur
after
 more protracted periods of caffeine abstinence than were
observed
 in the present study, which could be tested in future stud-
ies. Furthermore, these symptoms have a lower incidence (while
nevertheless
 resulting in signiﬁcant impairment for those affected)
than other caffeine withdrawal symptoms and thus may not be
observed
 when comparing group means (see Juliano and Grifﬁths,
2004). Given that a review of prior research suggests that nausea,
vomiting, and upset stomach are valid symptoms of caffeine with-
drawal
 (Juliano and Grifﬁths, 2004), future research with the CWSQ
should continue to examine these symptoms.
Some
 prior studies have shown a dose-dependent relationship
between caffeine consumption and the incidence and severity of
caffeine
 withdrawal symptoms (e.g., Evans and Grifﬁths, 1999).
For
 example, Ozsungur et al. (2009) found a relationship between
self-reported caffeine consumption and two of three withdrawal
factors,
 with individuals consuming more than 100 mg per day hav-
ing greater odds of reporting fatigue/headache and dysphoric mood
during
 caffeine abstinence than those consuming less than 100 mg
per day. A relationship between self-reported level of caffeine expo-
sure
 and the severity of caffeine withdrawal was not observed in
the
 present study. However, our participants were heavy caffeine
consumers (mean 550 mg per day) who all consumed more than
100
 mg per day. It has been shown that ≥ 100 mg caffeine per day is
sufﬁcient to produce physical dependence (Grifﬁths et al., 1990).
Thus
 the failure to show a relationship between daily caffeine
amount
 and caffeine withdrawal in the present study could be due
to a restriction of range. The large individual differences in caffeine
dose
 effects and elimination, as well as caffeine’s  ﬂat dose–response
curve could also account for the absence of a dose–response  rela-
tionship
 (Denaro and Benowitz, 1991; James and Rogers, 2005).
Future studies should evaluate the relationship between daily
caffeine exposure and withdrawal severity using a more diverse
sample
 
of
 
caffeine
 
consumers,
 
including
 
light
 
users
 
and
 
those
 
who
consume caffeine primarily from non-coffee sources. As predicted,
daily
 coffee consumers experienced greater withdrawal symptoms
than
 non-daily coffee consumers, even after controlling for aver-
age daily exposure to caffeine. It is possible that daily caffeine use
may
 be a more sensitive predictor of caffeine withdrawal symp-
toms than self-reported level of caffeine consumption especially
among
 heavy consumers of caffeine. Individuals with varying lev-
els of caffeine consumption may self-select into a daily user group
because
 of a history of experiencing withdrawal symptoms upon
caffeine abstinence.
The CWSQ was signiﬁcantly correlated with self-reported crav-
ings
 for caffeine and cravings for coffee. Craving was not included in
the CWSQ because there is insufﬁcient evidence at this time to sug-
gest
 that craving is a valid symptom of caffeine withdrawal. Future
administrations of the CWSQ could include items pertaining to caf-
feine
 craving. It is notable that craving for sweets was not associated
with scores on the CWSQ, which suggests that participants discrim-
inated between caffeine-related and non-caffeine related cravings.
Future
 studies should evaluate the relationship between caffeine
abstinence and caffeine craving and also investigate if cravings for
caffeine
 depend on an awareness of caffeine abstinence, expectan-
cies
 for abstinence (Huntley and Juliano, 2011), as well as other
factors (Drummond, 2001).
There
 are some limitations of the present study that should be
noted.
 The data presented herein reﬂect only the ﬁrst stages of val-
idation of the CWSQ, which as with all measures is an on-going
process.
 Future studies should evaluate the CWSQ using larger
and more diverse samples, including clinical samples. It is pos-
sible that individuals who are willing to participate in research
involving
 caffeine abstinence may experience fewer and less severe
withdrawal symptoms than those unwilling to participate (anec-
dotally,
 some individuals who inquire about our studies tell us
that
 they are simply not willing to abstain from caffeine). Further-
more,
 one population based study found that among respondents
who
 reported having abstained from caffeine for ≥ 24 h, those who
did so as an attempt to permanently stop caffeine use reported
much
 higher rates of caffeine withdrawal symptoms (Hughes et al.,
1998). Thus our data may underestimate the frequency or sever-
ity
 of withdrawal symptoms in the general population and/or
certain
 subgroups. Future studies should objectively validate caf-
feine abstinence using biochemical measures. Research with the
CWSQ
 should also involve longer periods of caffeine abstinence
(e.g., >24 h) and repeated assessment to evaluate the time course
of
 withdrawal. Test–retest  trials are needed to evaluate the sta-
bility of the measure and co-variation of withdrawal symptoms.
The
 CWSQ could also be used in research on caffeine withdrawal
among
 children and adolescents, which is sorely needed; especially
given the growing popularity of highly caffeinated energy drinks
among
 young people (Reissig et al., 2009). At this time, there is
little evidence that anxiety is a caffeine withdrawal symptom and
thus
 anxiety-related descriptors were not included in the present
investigation. However, caffeine can produce anxiogenic effects.
Thus,
 anxiogenic effects (e.g., anxiety, nervousness, and jitteriness)
should
 be assessed when caffeine withdrawal research involves
caffeine administration.
The
 ﬁndings from the present study suggest that caffeine
withdrawal is a multi-dimensional construct consisting of seven
discrete
 components. This contrasts with the four symptom classes
proposed
 in the DSM-IV-TR. As is intended with a DSM diagno-
sis worthy of future research, most of the controlled experimental
research
 on caffeine withdrawal has taken place in the seventeen
years since these research criteria were ﬁrst proposed. At this time
there
 exists a substantial empirical database characterizing the
caffeine withdrawal syndrome and documenting its clinical signif-
icance, and warranting its inclusion as a diagnosis in the DSM-5.
Data
 
from
 
the
 
present
 
investigation
 
suggest
 
that
 
the
 
research
 
diag-
nosis in the DSM-IV-TR should be modiﬁed to include additional

[Page 6]

The
 ﬁndings from the present study suggest that caffeine
withdrawal is a multi-dimensional construct consisting of seven
discrete
 components. This contrasts with the four symptom classes
proposed
 in the DSM-IV-TR. As is intended with a DSM diagno-
sis worthy of future research, most of the controlled experimental
research
 on caffeine withdrawal has taken place in the seventeen
years since these research criteria were ﬁrst proposed. At this time
there
 exists a substantial empirical database characterizing the
caffeine withdrawal syndrome and documenting its clinical signif-
icance, and warranting its inclusion as a diagnosis in the DSM-5.
Data
 
from
 
the
 
present
 
investigation
 
suggest
 
that
 
the
 
research
 
diag-
nosis in the DSM-IV-TR should be modiﬁed to include additional

[Page 6]
234 L.M. Juliano et al. / Drug and Alcohol Dependence 124 (2012) 229– 234
symptoms
 and should recognize that non-headache symptoms do
occur
 independent of headache. Caffeine withdrawal is a clinically
important phenomenon with the potential to affect large numbers
of
 individuals given the high rates of caffeine use in the United
States and elsewhere. The CWSQ and its future reﬁnements should
prove
 useful in facilitating the assessment and diagnosis of caf-
feine withdrawal and increasing our understanding of the caffeine
withdrawal
 syndrome.
Role of funding source
Funding
 for this study was provided by the College of Arts and
Sciences
 at American University. The College had no further role in
the study.
Contributors
Authors Juliano, Harrell, and Westerman contributed to the
study
 design, subject recruitment, and running of the subjects.
Authors Juliano and Huntley conducted the statistical analyses. All
authors
 
contributed
 
to
 
the
 
writing
 
of
 
the
 
manuscript.
Conﬂict of interest
No
 conﬂict declared.
Acknowledgements
We thank Sarah E. Moore and Peter G. Kardel for their assistance
with this project.
Appendix A. Supplementary data
Supplementary
 data associated with this article can be found, in
the online version, at doi:10.1016/j.drugalcdep.2012.01.009.

metadata.core_insight_en: The study developed a 23-item questionnaire called the Caffeine Withdrawal Symptom Questionnaire (CWSQ) to measure caffeine withdrawal symptoms. The questionnaire was reduced to 7 composite variables: Fatigue/drowsiness, Low alertness/difﬁculty concentrating, Mood disturbances, Low socia-bility/motivation to work, Nausea/upset stomach, Flu-like feelings, and Headache. The study found that daily coffee consumers experienced more severe withdrawal symptoms than non-daily consumers.

Hypnic headache and caffeine
Article  in  Expert Review of Neurotherapeutics · September 2012
DOI: 10.1586/ern.12.100 · Source: PubMed
CITATIONS
27
READS
3,884
2 authors:
Dagny Holle
University Hospital Essen
147 PUBLICATIONS   3,428 CITATIONS   
SEE PROFILE
Mark Obermann
Klinikum Weser-Egge
288 PUBLICATIONS   8,091 CITATIONS   
SEE PROFILE
All content following this page was uploaded by Mark Obermann on 26 May 2014.
The user has requested enhancement of the downloaded file.

[Page 2]
10.1586/ERN.12.100 1125ISSN 1473-7175© 2012 Expert Reviews Ltd
www.expert-reviews.com
Review
Hypnic headache (HH) is an uncommon pri -
mary headache disorder that tends to only affect 
patients older than 50 years of age. However, sin-
gle case reports also describe HH also in younger 
patients and even in children [1]. Patients usually 
wake up with a holocranial headache on more 
than 15 nights per month. Usually the headache 
lasts more than 15
 
min. As the headache often 
occurs at the same time at night, this headache 
entity is also referred to as an ‘alarm clock head-
ache’. In addition, HH attacks can also arise 
from day-time naps. More women than men are 
affected by this disease. Moderate migraneous 
features such as nausea or phono- or photo-pho-
bia might accompany the pain, and trigeminal 
autonomic symptoms such as lacrimation or rhi-
norrhea are missing in most cases 
[2]. In contrast 
to migraineurs, HH patients report that they get 
out of bed when they awake with headache and 
perform some motor activity [3]. Some patients 
take a shower, some eat, some read and some go 
for a walk. However, HH patients do not present 
with the kind of restlessness that can be observed 
in cluster headache patients (Box  1).
The underlying pathophysiology of HH is still 
mysterious. The circadian rhythmicity of head -
ache attacks is the most striking pathognomonic 
feature in HH, and it is thought to reflect pos -
sible hypothalamic involvement. Initially, some 
authors suggested HH to be a rapid eye move -
ment (REM) sleep disorder, but previously non-
REM (NREM) sleep-associated HH attacks 
have been also described 
[4,5]. Other authors 
have suggested that obstructive sleep apnea or 
nocturnal high blood pressure might cause HH, 
but these cases should be rather considered as 
secondary causes. HH can also occur secondary 
due to intracranial lesions [6–8] or ischemic stroke 
in the midrostral upper pons [9].
Controlled clinical trials investigating the 
efficacy of different treatment options are cur -
rently unavailable. One typical feature of HH 
is that caffeine is often helpful to relieve pain as 
an acute acting agent and also prevents pain due 
to its prophylactic properties when consumed at 
night before going to bed.
A significant connection between headache 
disorders and caffeine is quite well known. 
Caffeine-containing mixed analgesics are 
widely used in several headache disorders and 
are often effective. Chronic caffeine intake can 
induce withdrawal headache after discontinu -
ation, and overconsumption can also induce a 
headache itself.
Possible mechanisms of caffeine in  
pain regulation
Caffeine is probably the most frequently 
consumed psychoactive compound around the 
world. For example, regular coffee contains up 
to 200
 
mg per serving, brewed tea up to 120
 
mg 
and cola up to 70
 
mg. Caffeine is an antagonist 
of adenosine A1, A2A and A2B receptors [10], which 
are localized at multiple sites such as the spinal 
cord, the thalamus and other supraspinal sites 
[11]. Its analgesic properties seem to be mainly 
based on interaction with these receptors [12]. In 
higher concentrations, which normally cannot be 
reached through normal dietary intake, further 
pharmacological actions can be triggered such 
as inhibition of phosphodiesterase, Ca
2+ release 
and block of GABA A receptors [10].
Dagny Holle* and  
Mark Obermann
Department of Neurology, University of 
Duisburg-Essen, Essen, Germany
*Author for correspondence:  
Tel.: +49 201 723 84852  
Fax: +49 201 723 5542  
dagny.holle@uk-essen.de
Hypnic headache (HH) is a rare primary headache disorder primarily affecting patients over the 
age of 50 years. This headache entity occurs exclusively at night almost always at the same time. 
Imaging data as well as the strict circadian rhythm of headache attacks suggest a hypothalamic 
involvement in the underlying pathophysiology of this headache disorder. One interesting clinical 
feature about HH is its therapeutic response to caffeine as an acute as well as prophylactic agent. 
This review addresses the efficacy of caffeine in HH as well as in other headache disorders, and 
attempts to give insight into the analgesic properties of this substance. In addition, it depicts 
possible problems of caffeine consumption with regard to headache.
Hypnic headache and caffeine

Review
Intrinsic analgesic effects & interactions of caffeine
Most data on the intrinsic analgesic effects of caffeine result from 
preclinical data and animal experiments. Antinociceptive effects 
are generally observed at doses between 35 and 100 mg/kg 
[13]. 
Interestingly, some studies even reported hyperalgesia in these 
low systemic doses 
[14], potentially based on inhibition of spinal 
adenosine A1 receptors as A1 receptors do not seem to be involved 
in the antinociceptive effects of caffeine.

metadata.core_insight_en: Hypnic headache is a rare primary headache disorder primarily affecting individuals over 50 years old, characterized by a holocranial headache occurring more than 15 nights per month and lasting over 15 minutes. Caffeine is a common treatment option, with both acute and prophylactic properties. The underlying pathophysiology remains mysterious, with theories including hypothalamic involvement, REM/NREM sleep disorders, obstructive sleep apnea, nocturnal hypertension, and intracranial lesions.
metadata.core_insight_ko: 50세 이상의 환자에게 주로 발생하는 희귀한 일차성 두통 장애인 수면성 두통은 한 달에 15회 이상 발생하는 전반적인 두통을 특징으로 하며, 지속 시간이 15분을 넘습니다. 카페인은 급성 및 예방 효과가 있는 일반적인 치료 방법입니다. 그러나 그 기전은 아직 미스터리로 남아 있으며, 시상하부 관여, REM/NREM 수면 장애, 수면 무호흡증, 야간 고혈압, 뇌내 병변 등 다양한 이론이 제시되고 있습니다.

[Page 3]
 Expert Rev. Neurother.  12(9), (2012)1126
Review
Intrinsic analgesic effects & interactions of caffeine
Most data on the intrinsic analgesic effects of caffeine result from 
preclinical data and animal experiments. Antinociceptive effects 
are generally observed at doses between 35 and 100 mg/kg 
[13]. 
Interestingly, some studies even reported hyperalgesia in these 
low systemic doses 
[14], potentially based on inhibition of spinal 
adenosine A1 receptors as A1 receptors do not seem to be involved 
in the antinociceptive effects of caffeine. In contrast, the antino -
ciceptive properties of caffeine result mainly from its interaction 
with the adenosine A
2A and A 2B receptors, especially on periph -
eral action sites. Due to adenosine A 1 receptor antagonism, low 
doses of caffeine are able to inhibit the analgesic effects of several 
analgesics such as acetaminophen 
[14 ,15], amitriptyline [16–19], car-
bamazepine and oxcarbazepine [20,21], as well as venlafaxine [18]. 
These studies raise the question of whether caffeine influences 
the effectiveness of analgesic drugs.
Adjuvant analgesic effects of caffeine
Moderate doses of caffeine (10–35 mg/kg) lead to an augmentation 
of the analgesic effect of pain killers such as acetaminophen [22,23] 
and several nonsteroidal anti-inflammatory drugs [22,24–28] . Several 
different mechanisms have been proposed to be responsible for 
these adjuvant analgesic effects of caffeine. One study investigated 
the inhibitory effects of acetaminophen and caffeine on lipopoly-
saccharide-induced cyclooxygenase (COX) and prostaglandin E2 
synthesis in primary rat microglial cells 
[29]. Acetaminophen 
and caffeine led to a dose-dependent inhibition of prostaglan -
din E2 synthesis as well as an inhibition of COX enzyme activ -
ity. Interestingly, caffeine also induced an inhibition of COX-2 
protein synthesis. Adjuvant analgesic effects were observed when 
adding acetylsalicylic acid, salicylic acid and dipyrone. Different 
authors suggest the central amine system to be involved in the 
adjuvant analgesic effect of caffeine 
[29]. Locomotor activity of 
rats was investigated in hot-plate and formaline tests after systemic 
administration of caffeine to investigate the mode of action of 
this compound. In both tests a dose-related antino  ciception was 
observed. Anti-inflammatory action was not detected. Application 
of naloxone did not influence the effect of caffeine, which implies 
little or no involvement of endogenous opioids at the caffeine site 
of action. Phentolamine, prazosin and idazoxan enhanced the 
analgesic effect of caffeine, although each 
substance augmented different parts of the 
caffeine effect. The authors concluded that 
a-adrenergic receptors could be involved in 
the action of caffeine.
At moderate doses, results regarding the 
adjuvant effect of caffeine for the analgesic 
properties of morphine are diverse. Some 
authors describe an augmentation of opi -
oid effectiveness 
[30], whereas some authors 
detected an inhibition [31,32]. Higher doses 
of caffeine (75–100 mg/kg) lead to an aug-
mentation of opioid effectiveness, despite 
their own intrinsic properties 
[31,33].
Possible mechanisms of caffeine in sleep regulation
Caffeine as an antagonist of adenosine is known to have a major 
influence on sleep from daily experience. Adenosine is a purine 
nucleoside that is localized in all cells 
[34]. It is a key signalling 
molecule for sleep induction [35] and interacts mainly with the 
cerebral adenosine A2A receptor. The adenosine A2A receptors are 
present in high concentrations in the striatum, nucleus accum -
bens and olfactory bulb [36]. Animal experiments have shown 
that application of adenosine A 2A receptor agonists to the sub -
arachnoidal space can induce NREM sleep [37–40] . Huang et al. 
investigated the promotion of wakefulness after caffeine applica -
tion in mice [35]. Caffeine-induced wakefulness was observed in 
wild-type mice as well as in adenosine A1 receptor knockout mice. 
No effect was observed in adenosine A2A knockout mice, support-
ing the crucial role of this type of receptor in sleep regulation [41]. 
The caffeine-induced wakefulness appears, therefore, to strongly 
depend on its adenosine A
2A receptor antagonism.
Caffeine in the treatment of hypnic headache
Until now, approximately 200 HH cases have been reported 
in the literature. Due to the rareness of this disease, treatment 
recommendations are mainly based on expert opinion and small 
case series, as randomized placebo-controlled clinical trials have 
not yet been carried out. Almost all patients report that drinking 
a cup of strong black coffee in case of pain is effective in treating 
acute HH attacks at night. Drinking a cup of coffee before 
going to sleep is also effective in preventing HH attacks. In 
our own German HH cohort, 11 out of 14 patients reported 
caffeine to be an effective treatment option 
[3], which made it 
the most effective acute treatment option compared with all 
other reported treatment attempts. However, the efficacy rates 
reported in the literature are quite diverse. Many cases seem 
to have a very good treatment response with caffeine, but in 
some cases no relevant pain relief was reported. Many patients 
are afraid that this treatment might lead to the disruption 
of their natural sleep pattern, especially because many older 
patients are affected by insomnia anyway. However, caffeine-
induced sleep problems are only described in a few patients 
[42, 43]. Caffeine-containing analgesics seem to be effective in 
HH as well. Typically, patients report that caffeine-containing 
Box 1. Diagnostic criteria of hypnic headache according to the criteria 
of the International Headache Society classification.
A Dull headache fulfilling criteria B–D
B Develops only during sleep, and awakens patient
C At least two of the following characteristics:
 – Occurs >15 times per month
 – Lasts ≥15 min after waking
 – First occurs after the age of 50 years
D  No autonomic symptoms and no more than one of nausea, photophobia or 
phonophobia
E Not attributed to another disorder
Reproduced with permission from [2] .
Holle & Obermann

[Page 4]
1127
www.expert-reviews.com
Review
analgesics (e.g., paracetamol/acetaminophen and caffeine) lead 
to substantial pain relief during an acute HH attack, whereas 
analgesics alone have shown no influence on the headache.

metadata.core_insight_en: Hypnic headache is a rare primary headache disorder primarily affecting individuals over 50 years old, characterized by a holocranial headache occurring more than 15 nights per month and lasting over 15 minutes. Caffeine is a common treatment option, with both acute and prophylactic properties. The underlying pathophysiology remains mysterious, with theories including hypothalamic involvement, REM/NREM sleep disorders, obstructive sleep apnea, nocturnal hypertension, and intracranial lesions.
metadata.core_insight_ko: 50세 이상의 환자에게 주로 발생하는 드문 일차성 두통 장애인 수면성 두통은 한 달에 15회 이상 발생하는 전반성 두통을 특징으로 하며, 지속 시간이 15분을 넘습니다. 카페인은 급성 및 예방 효과를 가진 일반적인 치료 방법으로 사용됩니다. 그러나 그 병리 기전은 아직 명확하지 않으며, 시상하부 관여, REM/NREM 수면 장애, 수면 무호흡증, 야간 고혈압, 뇌내 병변 등 다양한 이론이 제시되고 있습니다.

text:
Typically, patients report that caffeine-containing 
Box 1. Diagnostic criteria of hypnic headache according to the criteria 
of the International Headache Society classification.
A Dull headache fulfilling criteria B–D
B Develops only during sleep, and awakens patient
C At least two of the following characteristics:
 – Occurs >15 times per month
 – Lasts ≥15 min after waking
 – First occurs after the age of 50 years
D  No autonomic symptoms and no more than one of nausea, photophobia or 
phonophobia
E Not attributed to another disorder
Reproduced with permission from [2] .
Holle & Obermann

[Page 4]
1127
www.expert-reviews.com
Review
analgesics (e.g., paracetamol/acetaminophen and caffeine) lead 
to substantial pain relief during an acute HH attack, whereas 
analgesics alone have shown no influence on the headache. 
In our own German cohort, caffeine-containing analgesics 
were effective in five out of nine patients 
[3]. However, mixed 
analgesics run the risk of the development of medication 
overuse headache (MOH). As many HH patients suffer from 
HH attacks almost every day, total doses of combination 
drugs may easily exceed the crucial amount. In addition, the 
analgesic compound used has been associated with further side 
effects (e.g., acetylsalicylic acid associated with: gastrointestinal 
bleeding), and contraindications other than caffeine treatment 
alone might apply. Therefore, intake of mixed analgesics should 
be avoided and intake of caffeine alone is recommended.
Interestingly, most patients discovered caffeine as an effec -
tive treatment option on their own before they were diagnosed 
with HH. Dramatic treatment response to caffeine seems to be a 
pathognomonic clinical feature of HH that may help clinicians 
to correctly diagnose this rare headache disorder.
Caffeine should always be considered as a first-line therapy, as 
it is effective in many patients and is associated with no major 
side effects and no relevant contraindications.
Other treatment options in hypnic headache
Due to the low prevalence of HH randomized placebo-controlled 
clinical trials, date on the efficacy of distinct drug treatments are 
still missing. Therefore, only class IV evidence is available, mainly 
based on experts’ opinion and clinical experience 
[44]. Similar to 
caffeine treatment, acute and prophylactic treatment regimens 
should also be distinguished.
Acute treatment options
Medication is considered as an acute treatment when taken in case 
of headache, relieving the pain rapidly. Before starting a medica-
tion, headache frequency needs to be evaluated. Some treatment 
options may be effective but may also be accompanied by major 
side effects when taken every day.
Triptans, especially in combination with caffeine, has been 
described as effective in some HH patients 
[42]. In our own 
German cohort, only four patients tried triptans as an acute 
medication, and it was effective in all of them 
[3]. In contrast, 
the majority of case reports and case studies in the current lit -
erature found a rather small efficacy rate of triptans [45–50] . As 
the duration of the headache is often short, a fast-acting agent 
(such as nasal spray or an orally disintegrating tablet) might 
be preferred, even though subcutaneous sumatriptan has not 
been effective in HH treatment 
[43]. To avoid side effects, no 
more than two doses of any triptan should be taken within 
24 h. Triptans can induce MOH when taken for more than 
10 days per month for >3 months. Migraineurs in particular 
are prone to develop MOH, and many HH patients have also 
suffered from migraine headache attacks in their younger years, 
although the coexistence of HH and an active migraine has been 
only reported in a few cases. Therefore, intake on more than 10 
days a month should be avoided. Triptans are contraindicated 
in patients with established heart disease, high blood pressure, 
peripheral vascular disease, impaired liver function, stroke and 
diabetes, which might exclude a substantial part of HH patients 
from this  treatment option.
Many substances that are used on a regular basis in other head-
ache disorders, especially trigeminal autonomic cephalalgias, have 
been shown to be ineffective in HH 
(Box  2).
Prophylactic treatment options
Medication is considered as a prophylactic treatment when taken 
on a daily basis to prevent the occurrence of headache attacks. 
Before starting prophylactic medication one should always take 
the elderly patient population of HH into account. These patients 
often suffer from different concomitant diseases, often develop 
side effects of prescribed drugs and have limited medication tol -
erance. As the majority of HH attacks is of mild-to-moderate 
intensity and mainly of short duration, patients are often more 
concerned with prophylactic treatment than symptomatic treat -
ment options 
[51]. They often complain about the high frequency 
of headache attacks (in most cases >15 per month), which lead to 
substantial sleep disturbances.
Numerous case reports and case studies have described the effi-
cacy of lithium in prophylactic treatment of HH 
[3,46, 47,49,52–67] . 
However, titration to the adequate blood level is difficult and 
many patients discontinued therapy due to side effects (e.g., 
tremor, diarrhea, increased thirst and polyuria). In our German 
cohort, more than half of the patients stopped lithium therapy 
in this regard 
[3]. In addition, further drug interactions need to 
be considered.
Topiramate (dosage 25–100 mg/day) was reported to be effec -
tive in some cases as a prophylactic HH treatment [47,63]. In our 
German cohort, five patients were administered topiramate, two 
of which reported it to be effective in preventing HH attacks 
[3].
In patients with unilateral HH disorders, indometacin (dos -
age 25–150 mg/day) might be a further treatment option. 
However, the reported treatment outcomes are rather diverse 
[3,42, 46, 48,50,51,61,65,67–75]. Gastrointestinal side effects are frequently 
reported and may lead to premature treatment discontinuation.
Single case reports and smaller case studies indicate the possible 
prophylactic effects of melatonin (dosage 3–5 mg/day) [48,70], 
amitriptylin (dosage 10–100  mg/day) [43, 45,47–50, 56] , oxeteron 
(180 mg/day [54]), botulinum toxin type A [45] and pregabalin 
(dosage 75–150 mg/day) [70]. Other reported treatment options 
seem to be ineffective or not sufficiently effective as prophylactic 
therapeutic options 
(Box  3).
Box 2. Ineffective or not sufficiently effective acute 
drug treatment in hypnic headache.

metadata.core_insight_en: Hypnic headache is a rare primary headache disorder primarily affecting individuals over 50 years old, characterized by a holocranial headache occurring more than 15 nights per month and lasting over 15 minutes. Caffeine is a common treatment option, with both acute and prophylactic properties. The underlying pathophysiology remains mysterious, with theories including hypothalamic involvement, REM/NREM sleep disorders, obstructive sleep apnea, nocturnal hypertension, and intracranial lesions.

text:
Gastrointestinal side effects are frequently 
reported and may lead to premature treatment discontinuation.
Single case reports and smaller case studies indicate the possible 
prophylactic effects of melatonin (dosage 3–5 mg/day) [48,70], 
amitriptylin (dosage 10–100  mg/day) [43, 45,47–50, 56] , oxeteron 
(180 mg/day [54]), botulinum toxin type A [45] and pregabalin 
(dosage 75–150 mg/day) [70]. Other reported treatment options 
seem to be ineffective or not sufficiently effective as prophylactic 
therapeutic options 
(Box  3).
Box 2. Ineffective or not sufficiently effective acute 
drug treatment in hypnic headache.
• Nonsteroidal anti-inflammatory drugs
• Acetaminophen 
• 100% oxygen inhalation
• Opioids 
• Indomethacin
• Ergotamine
Hypnic headache & caffeine

[Page 5]
 Expert Rev. Neurother.  12(9), (2012)1128
Review
Hypothalamic involvement in hypnic headache
The hypothalamus plays a key role in the regulation of sleep 
and wakefulness, mainly due to hypothalamic subsections such 
as the suprachiasmatic nuclei. Besides other important regula -
tion features, it is involved in pain control as it displays extensive 
afferent and efferent neuronal connections to nociceptive and 
antinociceptive brainstem nuclei. The strict association between 
sleep and HH lead to the assumption that hypothalamic changes 
could be involved in the pathophysiology of HH. This hypoth -
esis is supported by voxel-based morphometry data showing a 
significant reduction of gray matter within the hypothalamus 
of affected patients 
[101]. Some studies suggest that HH could 
be a REM sleep disorder as some polysomnography studies have 
reported a high association between the occurrence of HH attacks 
and REM sleep 
[43,48, 49,60]. More recent studies, however, have 
doubted this obligate association and show that HH attacks occur 
just as  commonly during NREM sleep stages 
[4,5,61].
Caffeine in the treatment of other headache disorders 
& non-headache disorders
Migraine & tension-type headache
Approximately 30 years ago, repeated clinical trials indicated that 
adding caffeine to analgesics such as aspirin, acetaminophen and 
ibuprofen reduced the analgesic need by 40% 
[76–80] . Clinical 
trials suggest that doses of 65 mg may be useful for analgesic 
enhancement. Caffeine-containing analgesics more often lead 
to pain relief in primary headache disorders than the analgesic 
compound alone 
[81]. Some studies even suggest an intrinsic anal-
gesic property of caffeine. Ward et al. investigated 53 patients 
with non-migraineous headache 
[82]. In a double-blind, placebo-
controlled multiple crossover design, four different treatments 
were compared: placebo, acetaminophen, two doses of caffeine, 
and a combination of caffeine and acetaminophen. The results 
indicated caffeine-independent analgesic effects. A different 
study investigated 301 patients with tension-type headache in 
a randomized, double-blind, parallel, multicenter, single-dose, 
and placebo- and active-control study design 
[80]. The following 
treatment groups were investigated: ibuprofen and caffeine, ibu -
profen alone, caffeine alone and placebo. According to previous 
data, caffeine showed intrinsic analgesic properties, although the 
effects were not as substantial as the combination of ibuprofen 
and caffeine.
Postdural puncture headache
Postdural puncture headache (PDPH) is a well-known headache 
entity that occasionally follows diagnostic or therapeutic dural 
punctures. According to International Headache Society criteria, 
PDPH usually develops less than 7 days after dural puncture 
[2]. 
A postural component is mandatory, meaning that the headache 
worsens within less than 15 min after getting in the upright posi-
tion and improves within <30 min when laying supine again. 
Oral or intravenous caffeine seems to be an effective treatment 
option in PDPH. It has been shown to be effective in postpar -
tum patients 
[83]. Despite the good response rates after caffeine 
treatment (75–80%), headache recurrence seems to be a major 
problem as the headache returns in most cases 
[84].
Caffeine leading to headache
Caffeine withdrawal
Caffeine withdrawal is commonly recognized as important 
lifestyle factor causing headache. It has been shown that not 
only do high intake doses above 600 mg/day cause withdrawal 
symptoms after discontinuation, but so do moderate or even 
low doses of 100 mg/day 
[85]. Headache usually starts after 
12–24 h following caffeine intake discontinuation and lasts 
approximately 1 week. The pain peaks between 24 and 48 h [86]. 
Caffeine withdrawal might be one factor of the often observed 
perioperative headache, as many surgeries require patients to fast 
[17,87]. Hampl et  al. investigated 40 patients that underwent a 
minor surgical procedure. Half of the patients were treated with 
caffeine tablets, the other half only received placebo tablets. In 
the placebo group 50% of the patients reported headache after 
surgery; in the caffeine group no one suffered from headache 
[88]. 
Similar effects can be seen with intake of caffeinated beverages as 
well as intravenous application of caffeine 
[89].
Medication overuse headache
Medication overuse headache (MOH) is a common cause of 
chronic headache. The prevalence in the general population in 
Western societies ranges between 1 and 2% 
[90]. MOH is defined as 
a chronic headache (≥15 days/month ≥3 months) that occurs after 
frequent intake of ergot derivatives, triptans, opioids or analgesics 
as acute treatment medication 
[2]. Simple analgesics should not be 
taken for ≥15 days/month, and mixed analgesics containing caf -
feine pose an even higher risk for inducing MOH compared with 
single-substance formulations and should therefore not be taken 
for ≥15 days/month. Caffeine seems to increase the risk of MOH 
development to a level similar to that of ergot derivatives and opi-
oids 
[2]. The distinct mechanisms of MOH occurrence are still 
enigmatic and the role of caffeine in this context remains unclear.
A different study investigated the correlation between chronic 
headache in children and adolescents and excessive caffeine con -
sumption due to caffeine-containing soft drinks intake [91]. Thirty 
six subjects (17 girls and 19 boys; mean age: 9.2 years; mean head-
ache duration: 1.8 years; soft drink intake: 1.5 l per day [192.88 mg 
caffeine]) participated in this study. After gradual withdrawal 
from caffeine-containing soft drinks, in 33 patients the chronic 
 headache disappeared in 33 patients.

Caffeine is a common treatment option, with both acute and prophylactic properties. The underlying pathophysiology remains mysterious, with theories including hypothalamic involvement, REM/NREM sleep disorders, obstructive sleep apnea, nocturnal hypertension, and intracranial lesions.

text:
Caffeine seems to increase the risk of MOH 
development to a level similar to that of ergot derivatives and opi-
oids 
[2]. The distinct mechanisms of MOH occurrence are still 
enigmatic and the role of caffeine in this context remains unclear.
A different study investigated the correlation between chronic 
headache in children and adolescents and excessive caffeine con -
sumption due to caffeine-containing soft drinks intake [91]. Thirty 
six subjects (17 girls and 19 boys; mean age: 9.2 years; mean head-
ache duration: 1.8 years; soft drink intake: 1.5 l per day [192.88 mg 
caffeine]) participated in this study. After gradual withdrawal 
from caffeine-containing soft drinks, in 33 patients the chronic

• Flunarizine
• Prednisone
• Benzodiazepines
• Gabapentine
• Verapamil
• b-blockers

Holle & Obermann

[Page 6]
1129
www.expert-reviews.com
Review
Expert commentary
From our point of view, drinking a cup of coffee in the evening 
is an effective, safe and cheap treatment option that should be 
attempted as a first-line therapy in the prophylactic treatment 
of hypnic headache. However, many patients do not accept this 
treatment option due to concerns of increased sleeplessness. 
Interestingly, this side effect has only been described in very few 
patients and does not seem to be as prevalent as patients and doc-
tors often think 
[42,71]. Patients should be encouraged to attempt 
caffeine at least once, as they could genuinely benefit from a strong 
cup of coffee before going to bed.
Caffeine-containing analgesics are also effective in acute head -
ache treatment in migraine and tension-type headache. However, 
they imply an increased risk of MOH compared with single-
substance analgesics and should, therefore, be avoided in higher 
dosages and taken sporadically.
High caffeine, especially in the form of coffee, tea and other 
types of caffeine-containing beverages, can induce withdrawal 
headache when caffeine intake discontinues. The caffeine 
effect varies individually (e.g., due to body size) and depends 
on the degree of tolerance. Therefore, a maximum threshold for  
caffeine intake cannot be recommended in general.
The mode of action of caffeine with regard to its analgesic 
properties is still not completely understood. Several associ -
ated mechanisms have been identified such as adenosine A
2A 
receptor antagonism, inhibition of prostaglandin E2 synthesis,  
inhibition of COX enzymes and involvement of the cerebral amine 
system. However, with regard to HH the therapeutic response to 
caffeine goes beyond the usual analgesic effects observed in other 
headache disorders.
Clinical and imaging data suggest hypothalamic involvement 
in the pathophysiology of HH.
Five-year view
Further neurophysiological and neuroendocrinological studies 
will illuminate the underlying pathophysiology of HH. Increased 
knowledge about HH will likely increase the estimated prevalence 
rates. Multicenter approaches will help to conduct placebo-con -
trolled clinical trials to improve treatment options of HH. More 
focus will be placed on caffeine-containing beverages in the con -
text of medication overuse headache and withdrawal headache, 
especially as many children are used to drinking large amounts 
of these drinks.
Financial & competing interests disclosure
The authors have no relevant affiliations or financial involvement 
with any organization or entity with a financial interest in or financial 
conflict with the subject matter or materials discussed in the manuscript. 
This includes employment, consultancies, honoraria, stock ownership 
or options, expert testimony, grants or patents received or pending, or 
royalties.
No writing assistance was utilized in the production of this manuscript
.
Key issues
• Owing to the rareness of hypnic headache (HH), placebo-controlled therapeutic studies remain unavailable. Therefore, therapeutic 
recommendations are exclusively based on case reports, smaller case studies and experts’ opinions.
• Caffeine appears to be an effective treatment option for HH, with only a few side effects.
• Caffeine can be used as an acute treatment option in case of nocturnal headache, as well as a prophylactic treatment option to avoid 
occurrence of HH.
• Caffeine use alone and the combination of caffeine with analgesic drugs are also effective in treatment of migraine, tension-type 
headache and postdural puncture headache.
• Adenosine is the natural agonist at the adenosine A
2A receptor, which is mainly responsible for sleep induction and sleep regulation. It 
is mainly localized in the striatum, nucleus accumbens and olfactory bulb.
• Caffeine is an antagonist of the adenosine A 2A receptor and shows adjuvant analgesic effects as well as intrinsic analgesic properties.
• Caffeine withdrawal can lead to strong headaches, which is sometimes even seen in children who drink caffeine-containing beverages 
on a daily basis.
• Caffeine-containing analgesics used as an acute treatment more often induce medication-overuse headaches than simple analgesics 
and should, therefore, be avoided in headache therapy.

text:
[Page 1]
REVIEW
published: 03 December 2019
doi: 10.3389/fneur.2019.01275
Frontiers in Neurology | www.frontiersin.org 1 December 2019 | Volume 10 | Article 1275
Edited by:
Uwe Reuter,
Charité Medical University of
Berlin, Germany
Reviewed by:
Mark Braschinsky,
University of Tartu, Estonia
Anish Bahra,
National Hospital for Neurology and
Neurosurgery (NHNN),
United Kingdom
*Correspondence:
Karl B. Alstadhaug
Karl.bjornar.alstadhaug@nlsh.no
Specialty section:
This article was submitted to
Headache Medicine and Facial Pain,
a section of the journal
Frontiers in Neurology
Received: 17 September 2019
Accepted: 18 November 2019
Published: 03 December 2019
Citation:
Alstadhaug KB and Andreou AP
(2019) Caffeine and Primary (Migraine)
Headaches—Friend or Foe?
Front. Neurol. 10:1275.
doi: 10.3389/fneur.2019.01275
Caffeine and Primary (Migraine)
Headaches—Friend or Foe?
Karl B. Alstadhaug 1,2* and Anna P . Andreou3,4
1 Nordland Hospital Trust, Bodø, Norway, 2 Institute of Clinical Medicine, The Arctic University of Nor way, Tromsø, Norway,
3 Headache Research, Wolfson CARD, Institute of Psychiatry, P sychology and Neuroscience, King’s College London,
London, United Kingdom, 4 The Headache Centre, Guy’s and St Thomas’, NHS Foundation Tr ust, London, United Kingdom
Background: The actions of caffeine as an antagonist of adenosine recept ors have
been extensively studied, and there is no doubt that both dai ly and sporadic dietary
consumption of caffeine has substantial biological effect s on the nervous system.
Caffeine inﬂuences headaches, the migraine syndrome in par ticular, but how is unclear.
Materials and Methods: This is a narrative review based on selected articles from
an extensive literature search. The aim of this study is to el ucidate and discuss how
caffeine may affect the migraine syndrome and discuss the po tential pathophysiological
pathways involved.
Results: Whether caffeine has any signiﬁcant analgesic and/or prophy lactic effect
in migraine remains elusive. Neither is it clear whether caf feine withdrawal is an
important trigger for migraine. However, withdrawal after chronic exposure of caffeine
may cause migraine-like headache and a syndrome similar to t hat experienced in
the prodromal phase of migraine. Sensory hypersensitivity however, does not seem
to be a part of the caffeine withdrawal syndrome. Whether it is among migraineurs
is unknown. From a modern viewpoint, the traditional vascul ar explanation of the
withdrawal headache is too simplistic and partly not conceivable. Peripheral mechanisms
can hardly explain prodromal symptoms and non-headache wit hdrawal symptoms.
Several lines of evidence point at the hypothalamus as a locu s where pivotal actions
take place.
Conclusion: In general, chronic consumption of caffeine seems to increa se the burden
of migraine, but a protective effect as an acute treatment or in severely affected patients
cannot be excluded. Future clinical trials should explore t he relationship between caffeine
withdrawal and migraine, and investigate the effects of lon g-term elimination.
Keywords: headache, caffeine, adenosine, dopaminergic, his taminergic, circadian, yawning, withdrawal
“I am not acquainted with any agents which equal these substances (coﬀee and tea, aa), in the power of
removing headache without leaving inconvenient results. And as their physiological action is so purely
cerebral, restoring the intellectual faculties, and ministeri ng to the sensations of well-being, as well as
lessening any sad emotions, we have here an adequate presumption, were any required, that this headache
is seated in the nerves, which are immediately related with th e molecular action of the brain.”
John Addington Symonds, the Goulstonian lecture for 1858 (
1)

[Page 2]
Alstadhaug and Andreou Caffeine and Primary (Migraine) Headaches
INTRODUCTION
It is well-know that caﬀeine can stimulate wakefulness, incr ease
concentration and decrease the sensation of fatigue (
2), but how
does it aﬀect one of the most common human agonies ( 3),
headaches? Caﬀeine is commonly used as analgesic adjuvant
for the acute treatment of pain. However, despite the ﬂatterin g
description of the eﬃcacy above, the general analgesic eﬀect of
caﬀeine seems at best modest (
4). Besides, chronic consumption
of it may have a ﬂip side, withdrawal may cause caﬀeine
withdrawal (
5, 6), a syndrome including symptoms such as
drowsiness, headache, mood-changes, diﬃculty focusing, n ausea
and muscle pain/stiﬀness ( Box 1). Even small amounts of
caﬀeine have been shown to suppress this ( 5). Headache
can occur independently of the other symptoms ( 7), and
Caﬀeine-withdrawal headache (Box 2), properly described in
the 1940s ( 8, 9), is recognized as an own diagnostic entity
by the International Classiﬁcation of Headache Disorders
(ICHD-3) (
10). Results from both experimental and clinical
studies indicate a high rate of caﬀeine withdrawal in the
modern society, that may even be underestimated (
5). The
real world extent and clinical (physiological and psychologi cal)
importance are not well-known ( 11). The dual eﬀects of
caﬀeine in headaches, relieving on one side and triggering on
the other side, make caﬀeine a very interesting substance in
headache pathophysiology research. Still, the prevailing the ory
of the withdrawal headache is basically a rebound vasodilat ion
due to caﬀeine’s vasoconstrictive eﬀect (
12), at large a too
simplistic theory that is not in conformity with modern views
of headache pathophysiology (
13). The caﬀeine withdrawal
syndrome, which includes symptoms suggestive of the prodromal
phase of migraine, is hardly of peripheral origin. Based on the
current established knowledge on migraine pathophysiology,
this narrative review aims to explore how caﬀeine, which has
profound biological eﬀect as an adenosine receptor antagonist
(
14), may inﬂuence pathways involved in headaches, with a
particular focus in migraine.
BOX 1 | Diagnostic criteria for Caffeine-withdrawal according to the
DSM-5.
A. Prolonged daily use of caffeine.
B. Abrupt cessation of caffeine use, or reduction in the amou nt of caffeine
used, followed within 24 h by 3 or more of the following sympto ms:
a) Headache.
b) Marked fatigue or drowsiness.
c) Dysphoric or depressed mood, or irritability.
d) Difﬁculty concentrating.
e) Symptoms of nausea, vomiting, or muscle pain/stiffness.
C. Clinically signiﬁcant distress or impairment in social, o ccupational, or other
important areas of functioning.
D. Not due to the direct physiological effects of a general me dical condition
and are not better accounted for by another mental disorder.
BOX 2 | Diagnostic criteria for Caffeine-withdrawal headache according to
the ICHD-3.
A. Headache fulﬁlling criterion C.
B. Caffeine consumption of >200 mg/d for > 2 weeks, which has been
interrupted or delayed.

metadata.core_insight_en: Caffeine has a substantial biological effect on the nervous system, but its impact on migraine headaches is unclear. Chronic consumption of caffeine may increase the burden of migraine, but it may also have a protective effect as an acute treatment or in severely affected patients.
metadata.core_insight_ko: 카페인은 신경계에 상당한 생물학적 영향을 미치지만, 편두통에 대한 효과는 불분명합니다. 만성적인 카페인 섭취는 편두통 부담을 증가시킬 수 있으나, 급성 치료나 중증 환자에서는 예방 효과도 있을 수 있습니다.

text:
b) Marked fatigue or drowsiness.
c) Dysphoric or depressed mood, or irritability.
d) Difﬁculty concentrating.
e) Symptoms of nausea, vomiting, or muscle pain/stiffness.
C. Clinically signiﬁcant distress or impairment in social, o ccupational, or other
important areas of functioning.
D. Not due to the direct physiological effects of a general me dical condition
and are not better accounted for by another mental disorder.
BOX 2 | Diagnostic criteria for Caffeine-withdrawal headache according to
the ICHD-3.
A. Headache fulﬁlling criterion C.
B. Caffeine consumption of >200 mg/d for > 2 weeks, which has been
interrupted or delayed.
C. Evidence of causation demonstrated by both of the followi ng:
1. Headache has developed within 24 h after last caffeine int ake
2. Either or both of the following:
a) Headache is relieved within 1 h by intake of caffeine 100 mg .
b) Headache has resolved within 7 days after caffeine withdr awal.
D. Not better accounted for by another ICHD-3 diagnosis.
MATERIALS AND METHODS
The article is based on unsystematic searches in PubMed
with terms like “caﬀeine and headache, ” “caﬀeine withdrawal, ”
“adenosine and headache, ” with more, and on own knowledge of
older and recent literature on migraine. A discretionary se lection
of publications was made.
RESUL TS AND DISCUSSION
Caffeine and Adenosine
Caﬀeine is a major constituent of coﬀee and tea, but also naturally
occur in guarana, cola nuts, cocoa, and several other plants (
2).
Soft drinks, energy drinks, and dietary supplements are also
important sources, in particular among the younger population
(
15). The typical level in an ordinary cup of coﬀee varies between
50 and 100 mg ( 2). After oral ingestion, caﬀeine is rapidly and
completely absorbed (99%), with peak plasma concentration
reached usually within an hour (
16, 17). Caﬀeine passes through
all biological membranes, including the blood-brain barrie r, and
is distributed in all body ﬂuids ( 18). It is metabolized by the
cytochrome P450 system, the isoenzyme CYP1A2 responsible for
90% of caﬀeine clearance, and in adults it has a typical half-li fe
of 5 h (
19). Numerous factors, including a broad and variable
genetic basis (20, 21), modify caﬀeine clearance.
The actions of caﬀeine in doses relevant to human
consumption (50 to several 100 milligrams) are through
antagonism of G protein-coupled purinergic (P1) receptors,
more speciﬁcally the adenosine receptors (ARs), preferentiall y
the A 1R and A 2AR (
18, 22, 23). The human genes encoding for
these two receptors are ADORA1 and ADORA2, respectively.
Single nucleotide polymorphisms (SNPs) in ADORA2 appear
to play a role in an individual’s subjective response to
caﬀeine (
24–26).
Adenosine is found in every cell in the form of adenosine
5′-diphosphate (ADP) or adenosine 5 ′-triphosphate (ATP). It is
continuously formed by breakdown of ATP and the physiological
eﬀects of adenosine are directly related to the metabolic acti vity
(
27). It modulates the activity of numerous cells, including mast
cells, smooth muscle cells, platelets, and neurons ( 28). In the
nervous system, adenosine appears to play an important role in
Frontiers in Neurology | www.frontiersin.org 2 December 2019 | Volume 10 | Article 1275

[Page 3]
Alstadhaug and Andreou Caffeine and Primary (Migraine) Headaches
modulating brain neurotransmitter release, locomotion, r eward,
sleep/wakefulness, cognition, and analgesia ( 14). Adenosine is
neither stored nor released as a classical neurotransmitte r, but
may exert marked eﬀects on neuronal excitability through G
protein-coupled adenosine receptors (AR) A 1, A2A, A2B, and
A3 in both the peripheral and the central nervous system. The
A1R is virtually found everywhere in the brain. In general
it mediates tonic inhibition, especially through inhibitio n of
glutamate release from presynaptic nerve endings (
27). The
A2AR is concentrated in dopamine-rich regions ( 18), especially
on striato-pallidal GABAergic neurons ( 23), where it exerts
excitatory eﬀects on neurons and contributes to inhibition o f
motor activity ( 29). The A 2AR actions are complicated by the
fact that it co-localize and form heteromeres with dopamine- ,
cannabinoid-, and glutamate receptors ( 23). The roles of A 2BR
and A3R are not as well understood, but it appears that adenosine
has a lower aﬃnity for these receptors compared to A 1R and
A2AR receptors. Their activation is more likely to happen under
conditions of hypoxia during which there is increased adenos ine
availability (30, 31).
The Caffeine’s Effects Are in General
Opposite to the Effects of Adenosine
The brain levels of adenosine in cats and rodents in resting
physiological condition have been estimated to 30–200 nM/L,
concentrations suﬃcient to activate A 1, A 2A and possibly A 3
receptors if numerous on the cells (
30, 31), but in most tissues
the adenosine signaling is not very prominent ( 14). Even low
concentrations of caﬀeine, such as 1–10 µM achieved after
consumption of a single cup of coﬀee, result in signiﬁcant
antagonism of adenosine A 1 and A 2A receptors and may result
in increased alertness (
32).
Sleep and Arousal
An important function of adenosine in the CNS is its involvement
in the sleep/arousal system. Adenosine has sleep-promoting
eﬀects (
33). During sustained and prolonged wakefulness the
extracellular adenosine accumulates in the basal forebrai n
cholinergic region, and it declines slowly during recovery sleep
(34). This rise of adenosine reduces the cortical activity by
direct A 1R modulation of the corticopetal system, the major
extrathalamic relay of the reticular ascending system (RAS) to the
cortex, and indirectly via A 2AR-modulation of the hypothalamus
(35). It has been shown, for instance, that infusion of a
speciﬁc A 2AR agonist into the subarachnoidal space, inducing
NREM sleep, will cause increased activity in the ventrolatera l-
preoptic (VLPO) area of the anterior hypothalamus and a
reduced activity of the tuberomammilary nuclei (TMN) in the
posterior hypothalamus as seen by increased number of Fos-
positive neurons (
36). Caﬀeine-induced wakefulness depends on
adenosine A2A receptors (37).

metadata.core_insight_ko: 카페인은 신경계에 상당한 생물학적 영향을 미치지만, 편두통에 대한 영향은 명확하지 않습니다. 만성적인 카페인 섭취는 편두통 부담을 증가시킬 수 있으나, 급성 치료나 중증 환자에서는 오히려 보호 효과를 보일 수도 있습니다.

text:
This rise of adenosine reduces the cortical activity by
direct A 1R modulation of the corticopetal system, the major
extrathalamic relay of the reticular ascending system (RAS) to the
cortex, and indirectly via A 2AR-modulation of the hypothalamus
(35). It has been shown, for instance, that infusion of a
speciﬁc A 2AR agonist into the subarachnoidal space, inducing
NREM sleep, will cause increased activity in the ventrolatera l-
preoptic (VLPO) area of the anterior hypothalamus and a
reduced activity of the tuberomammilary nuclei (TMN) in the
posterior hypothalamus as seen by increased number of Fos-
positive neurons (
36). Caﬀeine-induced wakefulness depends on
adenosine A2A receptors (37). It seems that blocking of A 2AR in
nucleus accumbens inhibits the GABAergic output to the later al
hypothalamus, the TMN and the locus coeruleus (LC), causing
activation and the major arousal eﬀect of caﬀeine (
38). Genetic
knockout models have shown that both A 1 and A 2A receptors
are involved in mediating the sleep-promoting properties of
adenosine in the brain (
39). Moreover, the arousal eﬀects of
caﬀeine seen in wild-type animals are blunted in ADORA2A-
knockout mice ( 38). It is therefore conceivable that adenosine
receptor ligands could be used as normal cognitive enhancers or
sleep promoters.
Pain
Experimental data indicate that caﬀeine at doses between 25
and 100 mg/kg have intrinsic antinociceptive eﬀects (
40). To
extrapolate data derived from animal experiments to humans,
it is generally assumed that giving 10 mg/kg caﬀeine to a rat
corresponds to giving 3.5 mg/kg (about two to three cups of
coﬀee) to a 70 kg human (
18), although such an assumption
may be premature given the higher metabolic rate of rodents
and hence the shorter half-life of caﬀeine in their system. Ba sed
on this assumption, caﬀeine at doses around 600–1,200 mg
may be needed to achieve anti-nociception in caﬀeine-naive
humans. As an adjuvant caﬀeine ( ≥ 65 mg) can potentiate the
analgesic properties of other medications (
4, 41) by 40% ( 42),
and there is a possibility that caﬀeine alone in such low doses
might have intrinsic analgesic properties for some types of
pain, such as headache. Ward et al. employed a double-blind
placebo-controlled crossover design to assess whether caﬀein e
alone (60 and 130 mg) has independent analgesic eﬀects on
non-migrainous headaches, and found equivalent eﬀects to
acetaminophen (
43). Caﬀeine, and combination analgesics with
caﬀeine may be used in tension-type headache, but frequent use
is not recommended due to the risk of developing medication-
overuse headache (
41). It is also used in hypnic headache ( 44)
and post-lumbar puncture headache (45), but probably not to the
analgesic eﬀect per se . In hypnic headache the eﬀect of caﬀeine
is claimed to go “beyond the usual analgesic eﬀects observed
in other headache disorders” (
44). Hypothalamic eﬀects, as
described in the next section, may play a role. The eﬀect
on post-lumbar puncture headache, which has “not conclusive
evidence” according to a Cochrane report from 2013 (
45), has
been attributed non-analgesic mechanisms, including adenosine-
mediated vasoconstriction (46, 47).
Analgesic properties of caﬀeine may be hard to reconcile
with analgesic eﬀects of adenosine. When the concentrations o f
adenosine increase during stressful conditions, including noxious
stimulation, it may reduce pain ( 48), and adenosine receptor
agonists produce antinociception in a variety of pain models
(
49). Mice lacking A 1R show signs of increased anxiety and
hyperalgesia, and the antinociceptive eﬀects of adenosine see n
in wild-type mice ( 50) cannot be shown ( 48). A 1R is present
on peripheral sensory nerve terminals and in lamina II of the
spinal cord, and it has been suggested that peripheral anti-
nociception achieved by activation of A 1R occurs via blocked
release of endogenous calciton gene-related peptide (CGRP)
(
51) and substance P ( 52). Under inﬂammatory conditions,
experimental data indicate that the analgesic eﬀect is by
reducing hypersensitivity through a central mechanism (
49,
53). This is in accordance with human studies, showing that
intrathecal injection of adenosine does not cause antinoci ception
to acute thermal or chemical stimuli, but reduce allodynia
from intradermal capsaicin injection (
53). Adenosine may also
increase pain mediated by A 2A receptors (28). Experimental data
Frontiers in Neurology | www.frontiersin.org 3 December 2019 | Volume 10 | Article 1275

[Page 4]
Alstadhaug and Andreou Caffeine and Primary (Migraine) Headaches
indicate that there are pro-nociceptive A 2ARs on the peripheral
nerve terminals ( 28, 54), and that blocking of these may cause
antinociception ( 40). Caﬀeine in doses normally consumed by
humans can probably act as an analgesic, partly through blockade
of A 2A receptor ( 40). Central dopaminergic mechanisms may
also be involved. Like other analgesics ( 55), caﬀeine increases
dopamine release ( 18), probably via inhibition of A 2AR ( 56).
Notably, A2A receptor antagonists did not aﬀect dural meningeal
vasodilatation caused by CGRP in a rat model ( 57). The recent
marketing of CGRP receptor antibodies and the development of
CGRP antagonists has been considered a major breakthrough
in the migraine ﬁeld (
58). Despite the fact that A 2AR does not
appear to be located in the spinal cord, a speciﬁc inhibition of
the activity of the intermediolateral cell column (the sympa thetic
system) was shown by Brooks et al., indicating that the A 2AR
may be located on presynaptic inhibitory terminals of descending
ﬁbers from higher brain centers (
59).
It is clear that both adenosine and blockade of adenosine
by caﬀeine may cause anti-nociception. Since the nanomolar
aﬃnities of adenosine for A 1R and A 2AR are almost the same,
this indicates a ﬁne balanced modulation of the pain processin g
(
30), making it very diﬃcult to predict the net eﬀects of caﬀeine
on nociception in humans.
Caffeine Overuse and Withdrawal
Caﬀeine causes increased well-being in small to moderate dos es
and its overuse has the potential to cause physical dependence
(
60).

metadata.core_insight_ko: 카페인은 신경계에 상당한 생물학적 영향을 미치지만, 편두통에 대한 효과는 불분명합니다. 만성적인 카페인 섭취는 편두통 부담을 증가시킬 수 있으나, 급성 치료나 중증 환자에서는 예방 효과도 있을 수 있습니다.

text:
It is clear that both adenosine and blockade of adenosine
by caﬀeine may cause anti-nociception. Since the nanomolar
aﬃnities of adenosine for A 1R and A 2AR are almost the same,
this indicates a ﬁne balanced modulation of the pain processin g
(
30), making it very diﬃcult to predict the net eﬀects of caﬀeine
on nociception in humans.
Caffeine Overuse and Withdrawal
Caﬀeine causes increased well-being in small to moderate dos es
and its overuse has the potential to cause physical dependence
(
60). The caﬀeine dependence syndrome has been recognized by
the World Health Organization as a behavioral disorders due
to frequent use of caﬀeine (
61). Considerable evidence suggests
that this is due to enhanced dopaminergic activity, especiall y
via blocking of A 2AR causing increased dopamine release in
the ventral striatum (nucleus accumbens) ( 26). However, there
is a compensatory up-regulation of the adenosine system,
causing increased functional sensitivity to adenosine dur ing
withdrawal (
5, 10). These molecular changes appear to increase
functional sensitivity to adenosine during caﬀeine abstine nce,
and play an important role in the behavioral and physiological
eﬀects produced by caﬀeine withdrawal (
5, 10). In humans,
caﬀeine withdrawal following chronic consumption may give
rise to a time-limited syndrome comprising of headache,
drowsiness, mood-changes, diﬃculty focusing, nausea, and
muscle pain/stiﬀness ( Box 1) (
5, 6, 62). Consequently, caﬀeine
consumption may be maintained to avoid withdrawal symptoms.
Caﬀeine-withdrawal headache is a headache developing within
24 h after regular consumption of caﬀeine in excess of 200
mg/day for more than 2 weeks, which has been interrupted.
According to the ICHD3 classiﬁcation, this type of headache
resolves spontaneously within 7 days in the absence of furthe r
consumption (
10).
Further, repeated exposure to caﬀeine may lead to rapid
development of tolerance, preferentially to the A 1-blocking
eﬀect, and in some cases it even may result in opposite eﬀects than
expected (
14). By drinking three to four cups of coﬀee regularly
around 50% A1 and A2A receptor occupancy can be achieved for
several hours, and many of the actions of caﬀeine are due to thi s
AR blockade (18).

The Migraine Syndrome
Migraine is a disorder characterized by recurrent attacks of
head pain associated with hypersensitivity to sensory stimuli
(
10) and when full-blown it involves diﬀerent phases ( 63). (a)
A prodromal or premonitory phase hours prior to the onset of
the headache with a broad range of symptoms ( Table 1), which
patients can reliably recognize, and thus predict the occurre nce
of a headache, (b) Transient neurological symptoms, known
as migraine aura (typically visual alterations), just before the
actual headache starts (in migraine with aura patients), (c)
An intense headache, typically involving only one site of the
head, accompanied by nausea, sensitivity to light, noise and
smells, (d) The postdrome phase following the resolution of the
headache and characterized mainly by fatigue and inability to
concentrate (
66). Understanding the mechanisms involved in
the transition from a headache-free to the headache state is
crucial in understanding the underlying cause of headaches and
the development abortive drugs. In many primary headache
disorders, but especially migraine (
67, 68), several external
factors have been reported to trigger this transition; stres s,
bright light and lack of sleep are probably the most commonly
reported (
69). The periodicity of migraine attacks strongly
indicates involvement of internal clock mechanisms in its
pathophysiology (
70).
Frontiers in Neurology | www.frontiersin.org 4 December 2019 | Volume 10 | Article 1275

[Page 5]
Alstadhaug and Andreou Caffeine and Primary (Migraine) Headaches
Does the Migraine Prodromal Syndrome and
Caffeine-Withdrawal Syndrome Share the Same
Symptoms?
The prodromal, or premonitory, phase of migraine is usually
deﬁned as the period 2–48 h prior to aura or migraine headache
with symptoms ( Table 1) indicating an attack. Thirty to 90%
of migraine patients report such a phase (
71). In a prospective
electronic diary study, Giﬃn et al. found that yawning, incr eased
emotionality and concentration diﬃculties (diﬃculty reading and
speaking) were the most reliable predictors ( 72). In another
study by Schoonman et al., where patients had to choose
among 12 speciﬁc premonitory symptoms, the most frequently
reported were fatigue, phonophobia and yawning. Increased
emotionality and concentration diﬃculties were reported by
almost one third (
10). Kelman found that tiredness, mood
change and gastrointestinal symptoms (nausea) were the most
frequent reported symptoms, and that yawning was rarely
reported (
69). However, there were speciﬁc questions about the
former categories, but none about the latter symptom. In a
prospective survey of 100 unselected patients, Quintela et al.

text:
found that yawning, incr eased
emotionality and concentration diﬃculties (diﬃculty reading and
speaking) were the most reliable predictors ( 72). In another
study by Schoonman et al., where patients had to choose
among 12 speciﬁc premonitory symptoms, the most frequently
reported were fatigue, phonophobia and yawning. Increased
emotionality and concentration diﬃculties were reported by
almost one third (
10). Kelman found that tiredness, mood
change and gastrointestinal symptoms (nausea) were the most
frequent reported symptoms, and that yawning was rarely
reported (
69). However, there were speciﬁc questions about the
former categories, but none about the latter symptom. In a
prospective survey of 100 unselected patients, Quintela et al. (
64)
found that anxiety, phonophobia, irritability, unhappiness an d
yawning were the most common reported prodromal symptoms,
whereas asthenia, tiredness, somnolence and concentratio n
diﬃculties were the most common symptoms reported in the
postdromal phase. Due to diﬀerent methodologies used to
identify symptoms, it may be diﬃcult to compare results from
diﬀerent studies, but interestingly, the most prevalent sympt oms
reported are much the same as the symptoms of the caﬀeine-
withdrawal syndrome. In Table 1 ﬁndings from the very good
and comprehensive review about caﬀeine withdrawal symptoms
made by Juliano et al. (
7) are compared with ﬁndings from two
of the mentioned surveys. These data strongly suggest that t he
same or similar pathophysiological pathways may be involved
in both the prodromal phase of migraine and the caﬀeine
withdrawal syndrome. However, sensory hypersensitivity, a
cardinal migraineous feature (
10), does not seem to be part of
the caﬀeine withdrawal syndrome. This may nevertheless be du e
to an underlying thalamocortical dysrhythmia ( 73), that appears
to be speciﬁc for migraine. The overlap of symptoms may also be
due to the fact that caﬀeine withdrawal may act as a trigger of a
migraine attack in migraine patients?
Is the Caffeine Withdrawal Headache Similar to
Migraine?
Headache as a caﬀeine withdrawal symptom has been frequently
studied (
7), and in some case reports and experimental
studies it has been characterized. However, premorbid primar y
headache syndromes, even in otherwise solid papers ( 74), are
seldom reported. It seems though that subjects with a history
of frequent headaches (
75) are at increased risk, and that
migraineurs and subjects who experience withdrawal headach e
share some comorbidity such as major depression and anxiety
(
5). To “produce experimental headaches which would be
more physiological than experimental histamine and nitrite
headaches, ” Dreisbach in 1940 gave a number of non-habitual
coﬀee drinkers capsules of 10–12 grains (650–780 mg) of caﬀeine
daily for 1 week and then withdrew the treatment. On the
day of withdrawal, almost all developed a moderate to severe
headache. In migraineurs, “typical migraine syndromes” ens ued
(
8). Unfortunately, the report does not give suﬃcient data
for valid interpretation. Three years later, however, he and a
colleague reported 38 similar trials in 24 persons of whom 5 had
migraine. In 21 of the trials the subjects experienced their w orst
headache ever, in 11 it was deﬁnite but not severe, and in 6 the re
was slight or no headache. All of the migraineours experience d
caﬀeine withdrawal headache “quite diﬀerent from the migrain e
syndrome.” It is reason to question this statement. The head ache
was accompanied by nausea in 4 and vomiting in 1. In 4
subjects the headache was consistently accompanied by serous
rhinorrhea. This may indicate cranial autonomic symptoms,
frequently seen in migraine (
76). No information about other
accompanying symptoms was given. It was argued that 2 subjects
had headache localized to the opposite side of the head from
their usual migraine. In one subject, who used to have left-sid ed
frontal migraine, it occurred bilaterally and was localized in the
occipital region. Our clinical experience, however, conﬁrms that
is not unlikely for migraine to switch sides or to be bilateral rather
than unilateral. Scotomas, regularly experienced by 3 migrai ne
patients, did not occur. Scotoma, is a symptom usually described
in migraine with aura patients and to date no headache-induce d
substance has been found to consistently trigger migraine aur a.
Further, two of the migraineurs had migraine attacks on the
height of the caﬀeine stimulation, and in one of these subjects this
reoccurred in a second trial. In these trials, and in later studies the
headache is described to typically evolve gradually (
60, 77, 78),
being diﬀuse (79), throbbing (9), severe (60, 80), intensiﬁed with
exercise ( 77), and V alsalva manoeuver ( 9) and having a mean
duration for 2–3 days ( 81).
Despite being claimed that caﬀeine-related headache has a
non-migrainous clinical presentation ( 82), the description of
caﬀeine withdrawal headache given in the literature is not
very diﬀerent from migraine. With the high prevalence of
migraine (
83), and the lack of information on pre-existing
headache in many studies taken into consideration, there see ms
to be a clear shortcoming in the knowledge when it comes to
separating caﬀeine withdrawal as a migraine trigger from caﬀeine
withdrawal headache per se.
Does Caffeine Cause Headaches?
Caﬀeine itself is rarely reported as a trigger of migraine (
68),
but as noticed by Driesbach it probably has the potential ( 9). As
an important cause of medication-overuse headache (84) caﬀeine
has been strongly incriminated, partly due to the withdrawal
syndrome encouraging patients to continue their overuse (
85–
87). In a double blinded, randomized, placebo-controlled, 12
week crossover study of 45 healthy subjects who habitually
consumed 4 to 6 cups of coﬀee a day (
81),withdrawal caused
headaches during the ﬁrst or second day in 19 (42%) ( 81). The
ratio of subjects reporting headaches during caﬀeine weeks an d
during non-caﬀeine weeks was 1. However, what is obvious, but
not commented by the authors, is that when the withdrawal
weeks (week 1 and 7) are removed the ratio increases to
7.7. Subjects also reported improved sleep during the non-
caﬀeine weeks.
